Chapter 1: GaN Transistor Fundamentals for RF Power Amplifiers

Comprehensive Analysis Based on IFX Tx_Baseline Project

PA Design Reference Manual

2026-02-02

1 Introduction

1.1 Overview

This chapter provides a comprehensive foundation in GaN (Gallium Nitride) transistor technology for RF power amplifier (PA) design, with extensive evidence drawn from the IFX Tx_Baseline project (2022-2023). The chapter bridges theoretical principles with practical implementation, documenting the systematic transistor downselection process that guided real-world PA module development.

1.1.1 Chapter Objectives

  1. Establish GaN Technology Foundation
    • Understand GaN HEMT device physics and operating principles
    • Compare GaN advantages versus legacy technologies (LDMOS, GaAs)
    • Connect material properties to RF performance characteristics
  2. Master RF Characterization Methods
    • Small-signal S-parameter measurements and interpretation
    • Large-signal load-pull optimization techniques
    • Linearity and efficiency trade-off analysis
  3. Document Systematic Downselection
    • Learn Design of Experiments (DOE) methodology
    • Analyze 9+ transistor candidates across multiple technologies
    • Understand weighted selection criteria for real-world applications
  4. Apply Practical Design Knowledge
    • Biasing strategies for linearity versus efficiency optimization
    • Matching network topologies and design procedures
    • Stability analysis and thermal management considerations

1.1.2 Context: IFX Tx_Baseline Project

Project Scope: - Timeframe: 2022-2023 - Objective: Develop optimized 5G NR power amplifier module (PAM_B) - Application: Infrastructure base station transmitter - Frequency Band: 3.3-3.8 GHz (5G n77/n78) - Systematic Approach: 17+ DOE builds exploring device size, technology, bias, and matching

Key Artifacts Referenced: - 80+ technical documents including specifications, design reviews, measurements, and validation reports - 9 DOE simulation builds with systematic parameter variation - Comprehensive measurement data validating design methodology - Final baseline results demonstrating specification compliance

This chapter transforms project-specific learnings into generalizable PA design principles, providing readers with both theoretical understanding and evidence-based practical guidance.

1.2 Learning Outcomes

Upon completing this chapter, you will be able to:

  1. Explain GaN Device Physics
    • Describe AlGaN/GaN HEMT structure and 2DEG formation
    • Interpret DC I-V characteristics and key operating parameters
    • Relate material properties to RF performance advantages
  2. Perform RF Characterization
    • Conduct S-parameter measurements and extract stability factors
    • Execute load-pull measurements to optimize impedances
    • Measure and interpret linearity metrics (IM3, ACPR, EVM)
  3. Execute Transistor Downselection
    • Design systematic DOE exploration plans
    • Apply weighted selection criteria balancing multiple metrics
    • Document trade-off analysis and selection rationale
  4. Design Practical PA Circuits
    • Calculate optimal bias points for target operating class
    • Design input/output matching networks with harmonic control
    • Ensure unconditional stability across all frequencies
    • Implement thermal management strategies
  5. Validate with Simulation
    • Build transistor models from measurement data
    • Correlate simulated versus measured performance
    • Predict PA behavior across operating conditions

1.3 Roadmap

1.3.1 Section Organization

Section 1.1 (Introduction): Context and motivation for GaN technology adoption in modern RF systems.

Section 1.2 (GaN Device Physics): Fundamental operating principles of AlGaN/GaN HEMTs, 2DEG formation, and DC characteristics.

Section 1.3 (RF Characterization): Small-signal and large-signal measurement techniques essential for PA design.

Section 1.4 (Downselection Process): ⭐ CORE SECTION - Systematic DOE methodology with IFX Tx_Baseline evidence demonstrating how to select optimal transistors from multiple candidates.

Section 1.5 (Practical Design): Bias networks, matching topologies, stability analysis, and layout considerations.

Section 1.6 (Modeling & Simulation): Transistor model extraction, validation, and simulation tool usage.

Section 1.7 (Reliability & Packaging): Thermal management, reliability screening, and package selection (Minipac V2).

Section 1.8 (Summary): Key takeaways, best practices checklist, and transition to Chapter 2 (Multi-Stage PA Design).

1.4 Prerequisites

1.4.1 Assumed Knowledge

Readers should have familiarity with:

  • Electrical Engineering Fundamentals:
    • AC/DC circuit analysis
    • Impedance matching concepts
    • S-parameters and Smith charts
    • Basic semiconductor physics
  • RF/Microwave Basics:
    • Transmission line theory
    • Distributed elements (microstrip, stripline)
    • Gain, efficiency, and linearity definitions
    • Frequency-domain measurements (VNA usage)
  • Simulation Tools (helpful but not required):
    • ADS (Advanced Design System) or similar
    • Load-pull simulation capability
    • Linear/nonlinear analysis methods

1.4.2 Recommended Background Reading

For readers needing refreshers:

  1. RF/Microwave Circuits:
    • Pozar, “Microwave Engineering” (Chapters 2, 4, 12)
    • Gonzalez, “Microwave Transistor Amplifiers”
  2. GaN Device Physics:
    • Mishra & Shen, “Nitride Semiconductor Devices”
    • Pengelly et al., “A Review of GaN on SiC HEMTs”
  3. Power Amplifier Design:
    • Cripps, “RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Communications”
    • Grebennikov, “RF and Microwave Power Amplifier Design”

2 GaN Device Physics

2.1 1.2.1 AlGaN/GaN HEMT Structure

2.1.1 Device Cross-Section

The AlGaN/GaN High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT) exploits heterojunction engineering to achieve superior RF performance. The fundamental structure consists of:

×

📖 Heterojunction Engineering

Definition: The practice of joining two different semiconductor materials with different bandgaps to create unique electrical properties at their interface.

In GaN HEMTs: AlGaN (wider bandgap, ~4.0 eV) is grown on GaN (narrower bandgap, 3.4 eV), creating a conduction band discontinuity that forms a quantum well where the 2DEG accumulates.

Why It Matters: This interface engineering creates high-density, high-mobility electron channels without requiring doping, resulting in superior RF performance compared to conventional MOSFETs.

Key Layers:

  1. Gate Electrode: Schottky contact controlling channel conductance
  2. AlGaN Barrier Layer (~20-30 nm):
    • Higher bandgap (3.8-4.2 eV) than GaN
    • Creates conduction band discontinuity
    • Induces piezoelectric and spontaneous polarization
  3. GaN Channel Layer (~1-2 μm):
    • Wide bandgap (3.4 eV)
    • Location of 2DEG formation
    • Undoped or lightly doped
  4. Buffer Layers:
    • Transition layers (AlGaN grading, AlN nucleation)
    • Isolate channel from substrate
    • Suppress short-channel effects
  5. Substrate:
    • SiC (Silicon Carbide) - Best thermal conductivity
    • Si (Silicon) - Cost-effective, larger wafers
    • GaN - Lattice-matched, premium performance

2.1.2 Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)

×

📖 Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)

Definition: A quantum-mechanical phenomenon where electrons are confined to move freely in only two dimensions (along the interface) but are tightly confined in the third dimension (perpendicular to interface).

How It Forms in GaN: At the AlGaN/GaN heterointerface, the combination of polarization-induced electric fields and conduction band offset creates a triangular potential well. Electrons accumulate in this well, forming a sheet of charge just a few nanometers thick.

Key Properties:

  • High sheet density: ns = 1-2 × 1013 cm-2 (10× higher than GaAs)
  • High mobility: μ = 1500-2000 cm²/V·s at room temperature
  • No ionized impurity scattering (undoped channel)

Why It Matters for RF: The 2DEG enables very high current densities with low resistance, critical for high-power RF amplification.

Formation Mechanism:

The 2DEG is a quantum well of high-mobility electrons confined at the AlGaN/GaN interface. It forms due to:

\[ n_s = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E_{piezo}}{q} + \frac{\Delta E_C}{q d} \]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(n_s\) [cm⁻²] = Two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) sheet density, number of electrons per unit area at interface
  • \(\varepsilon_0\) [F/m] = Permittivity of free space = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m (fundamental constant)
  • \(\varepsilon_r\) [dimensionless] = Relative permittivity of AlGaN barrier layer (typically 9-10)
  • \(E_{piezo}\) [V/cm] = Piezoelectric polarization-induced electric field from strain (lattice mismatch between AlGaN and GaN)
  • \(q\) [C] = Elementary charge = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (fundamental constant)
  • \(\Delta E_C\) [eV] = Conduction band offset at AlGaN/GaN heterointerface (typically 0.3-0.5 eV)
  • \(d\) [nm] = AlGaN barrier layer thickness (typically 15-30 nm)

Physical Interpretation:

This equation has two terms representing different physical mechanisms:

  1. First term \(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E_{piezo}}{q}\):
    • Electrons induced by piezoelectric polarization
    • Strain from lattice mismatch (AlGaN has smaller lattice constant than GaN) creates electric field
    • This field pulls electrons to interface
    • Dominant contribution (~70-80% of total \(n_s\))
×

📖 Piezoelectric Polarization

Definition: Electric polarization (charge separation) that occurs in certain crystals when mechanical stress/strain is applied, or vice versa.

In GaN HEMTs: The lattice mismatch between AlGaN and GaN layers creates tensile/compressive strain. This strain induces strong electric fields (10⁶-10⁷ V/cm) normal to the interface, attracting electrons from the AlGaN to form the 2DEG.

Key Point: GaN (wurtzite structure) has exceptionally strong piezoelectric coefficients (~5-10× larger than GaAs), making this the dominant mechanism for 2DEG formation.

Design Control: Al composition in AlxGa1-xN (typically x=0.2-0.3) directly controls strain → polarization → 2DEG density.

  1. Second term \(\frac{\Delta E_C}{q d}\):
    • Electrons from spontaneous polarization and band offset
    • Wurtzite crystal structure of GaN has built-in polarization
    • Conduction band discontinuity creates quantum well
    • Secondary contribution (~20-30% of total \(n_s\))

Design Implications: - Higher Al content in AlGaN → more strain → higher \(E_{piezo}\) → higher \(n_s\) → more current - Thinner barrier \(d\) → higher field → higher \(n_s\), but increases gate leakage - Typical result: \(n_s\) = 1-2 × 10¹³ cm⁻² (10× higher than GaAs HEMTs)

Figure 1.1: GaN HEMT Structure - Band Diagram, Cross-Section, Top View, and Schematic Symbol

Key Properties:

Parameter Typical Value Significance
Sheet Density (\(n_s\)) 1-2 × 10¹³ cm⁻² High current capability
Electron Mobility (\(\mu\)) 1500-2200 cm²/V·s Fast switching, high gain
Sheet Resistance (\(R_{sh}\)) 300-600 Ω/□ Low on-resistance
2DEG Confinement <5 nm Quantum effects dominant

Advantages:

✅ No Doping Required: 2DEG forms naturally from polarization → fewer defects, higher mobility
✅ High Charge Density: 10× higher than GaAs HEMTs → more current per unit width
✅ Confined Channel: Reduces short-channel effects, enables scaling
✅ Temperature Stability: Polarization-induced, less sensitive to temperature than doping

2.1.3 Technology Comparison: GaN vs. LDMOS vs. GaAs

To appreciate GaN’s advantages, it’s essential to compare it with legacy RF power technologies.

Cross-Sectional Comparison: GaN HEMT, LDMOS, and GaAs HEMT Structures

Key Structural Differences:

  1. GaN HEMT:
    • Heterojunction (AlGaN/GaN) with polarization-induced 2DEG
    • No doping required in channel
    • Vertical structure with excellent heat dissipation
  2. LDMOS:
    • Lateral silicon MOSFET with extended drain
    • Doped channel (p-body with n+ source/drain)
    • Gate oxide controls surface inversion layer
    • Lower power density due to lower breakdown field
  3. GaAs HEMT:
    • Similar heterojunction concept but modulation-doped
    • Lower bandgap limits voltage capability
    • Semi-insulating substrate limits thermal performance
Performance Comparison: GaN HEMT vs. GaAs HEMT vs. LDMOS
RF Power Transistor Technologies
Parameter GaN HEMT GaAs HEMT LDMOS
Bandgap (eV) 3.4 1.43 1.12 (Si)
Breakdown Field (MV/cm) 3.3 0.4 0.3
Electron Saturation Velocity (×10⁷ cm/s) 2.5 2.0 1.0
Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) 130-180 (SiC) 46 150
2DEG/Channel Sheet Density (×10¹³ cm⁻²) 1.0-2.0 0.8-1.5 N/A (surface)
Electron Mobility (cm²/V·s) 1500-2200 6000-9000 400-600
Sheet Resistance (Ω/□) 300-600 200-400 N/A
Power Density (W/mm) 5-10 1-3 1-2
Operating Voltage (V) 28-50 8-12 28-50
Typical fT (GHz) 30-70 50-100 5-15
Typical fmax (GHz) 100-200 150-300 20-50
Relative Cost High Medium-High Low

Why GaN Wins for RF Power:

Advantage GaN Benefit
High Breakdown Field 10× higher than Si/GaAs → operate at higher voltages → higher RF power
Wide Bandgap Lower leakage, high-temperature operation (up to 200°C junction)
High 2DEG Density More current per mm → smaller devices for same power
Excellent Thermal SiC substrate dissipates heat effectively → prevents thermal runaway
High Saturation Velocity Fast switching → high gain at microwave frequencies

This makes GaN the technology of choice for modern base station PAs operating at 2-6 GHz with 28-48V supplies.

2.2 1.2.2 Operating Principles

2.2.1 Charge Control and Modulation

The gate voltage modulates the 2DEG density by controlling the depletion region:

\[ I_D = q \mu n_s(V_{GS}) W \frac{dV_{ch}}{dx} \]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(I_D\) [A] = Drain current (total current flowing from drain to source through channel)
  • \(q\) [C] = Elementary charge = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
  • \(\mu\) [cm²/(V·s)] = Electron mobility in 2DEG (1500-2200 cm²/(V·s) for GaN, temperature dependent)
  • \(n_s(V_{GS})\) [cm⁻²] = 2DEG sheet density as function of gate voltage (modulated by gate)
  • \(W\) [mm] = Total gate width (device periphery, e.g., 12mm for large PA device)
  • \(\frac{dV_{ch}}{dx}\) [V/cm] = Channel voltage gradient along x-direction (drain-to-source axis)
  • \(V_{ch}\) [V] = Channel voltage at position x
  • \(x\) [cm] = Position along channel from source (x=0) to drain (x=L)

Physical Interpretation: - This is the drift-diffusion equation for 2DEG current - Current is proportional to: charge density (\(q n_s\)) × mobility (\(\mu\)) × electric field (\(dV_{ch}/dx\)) × width (\(W\)) - Gate voltage \(V_{GS}\) controls \(n_s\) → modulates drain current → transconductance - In saturation region: channel pinches off at drain end, but current continues via velocity saturation

Operating Regions:

  1. Linear/Triode Region (\(V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}\)):
    • Channel acts as voltage-controlled resistor
    • \(I_D \propto V_{DS}\) (ohmic behavior)
    • Used for switches and mixers
  2. Saturation Region (\(V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}\)):
    • Channel pinches off at drain end
    • \(I_D\) independent of \(V_{DS}\)
    • RF amplifier operation region
    • Transconductance: \(g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\) is maximum
  3. Breakdown Region (\(V_{DS} > BV_{DSS}\)):
    • Avalanche or impact ionization
    • Gate leakage increases dramatically
    • MUST BE AVOIDED in PA operation

2.2.2 Transconductance and Current Control

Transconductance (\(g_m\)) determines gain and frequency response:

\[ g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg|_{V_{DS}=const} \approx \mu \frac{W}{L} C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) \]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(g_m\) [S or mS/mm] = Transconductance, rate of change of drain current with gate voltage (“trans” = across, conductance)
  • \(\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\) [A/V = S] = Partial derivative notation: change in \(I_D\) for small change in \(V_{GS}\) while holding \(V_{DS}\) constant
  • \(V_{DS}\) [V] = Drain-source voltage (held constant during measurement)
  • \(\mu\) [cm²/(V·s)] = Electron mobility in channel
  • \(W\) [mm] = Gate width (device periphery)
  • \(L\) [µm] = Gate length (typically 0.25-1.0 µm for RF GaN HEMTs)
  • \(C_{ox}\) [F/cm²] = Gate oxide/barrier capacitance per unit area = \(\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 / d\)
  • \(V_{GS}\) [V] = Gate-source voltage (bias point)
  • \(V_{th}\) [V] = Threshold voltage (typically -6 to -3 V for depletion-mode GaN)

Physical Interpretation: - \(g_m\) represents small-signal gain: how effectively gate voltage controls drain current - Higher \(g_m\) → higher RF gain (voltage gain \(A_v \propto g_m \times R_L\)) - Peak \(g_m\) occurs near threshold voltage plus 1-2V - Units: [S] = Siemens = 1/Ω = A/V, often expressed as mS/mm (normalized by gate width) - Approximation assumes long-channel MOSFET model; GaN HEMTs may deviate due to velocity saturation

Design Implications: - Class A bias: Set \(V_{GS}\) at peak \(g_m\) for maximum gain - Class AB bias: Slightly below peak \(g_m\) for efficiency-gain trade-off - Larger W/L ratio → higher \(g_m\) → higher gain, but also higher capacitance

GaN Advantages: - High \(g_m\) (200-500 mS/mm) due to high mobility and charge density - Enables high gain at high frequencies - Supports wideband operation

2.2.3 High-Frequency Performance

Key Figures of Merit:

  1. Current-Gain Cutoff Frequency (\(f_T\)): \[ f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})} \approx \frac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L_g} \]

    Variable Definitions:

    • \(f_T\) [Hz or GHz] = Current-gain cutoff frequency, frequency where short-circuit current gain \(|h_{21}|\) = 1 (0 dB)
    • \(g_m\) [S] = Transconductance (DC or low-frequency value)
    • \(C_{gs}\) [pF] = Gate-source capacitance (intrinsic + overlap, typically 0.5-2 pF/mm)
    • \(C_{gd}\) [pF] = Gate-drain capacitance (Miller capacitance, typically 0.05-0.2 pF/mm)
    • \((C_{gs} + C_{gd})\) [pF] = Total input capacitance that must be charged/discharged
    • \(v_{sat}\) [cm/s] = Electron saturation velocity (≈ 2.5 × 10⁷ cm/s for GaN)
    • \(L_g\) [µm] = Gate length (physical dimension of gate contact)
    • \(2\pi\) [dimensionless] = Converts frequency to angular frequency

    Physical Interpretation:

    • \(f_T\) is the frequency where transistor can no longer provide current gain
    • First form \(\frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}\): time constant perspective
      • Larger \(g_m\) → faster current response → higher \(f_T\)
      • Larger capacitance → slower charging → lower \(f_T\)
      • Gate capacitance acts as RC time constant limiting speed
    • Second form \(\frac{v_{sat}}{2\pi L_g}\): transit time perspective
      • Electrons must transit gate length at saturation velocity
      • Transit time \(\tau = L_g / v_{sat}\)
      • \(f_T = 1 / (2\pi \tau)\) = fundamental speed limit
    • Shorter gate length → faster transit → higher \(f_T\) (but harder to fabricate)

    Design Implications:

    • Typical GaN: \(f_T\) = 30-100 GHz (depends on gate length: 0.25 µm → 80 GHz, 1.0 µm → 25 GHz)
    • For RF PA at 3.5 GHz: need \(f_T\) > 10× operating frequency → \(f_T\) > 35 GHz
    • Determined by gate length and saturation velocity
    • GaN advantage: High \(v_{sat}\) enables high \(f_T\) even with longer gates (easier manufacturing)

  2. Maximum Oscillation Frequency (\(f_{max}\)): \[ f_{max} = \frac{f_T}{2\sqrt{R_g(R_{ds} + R_s) \cdot (2\pi f_T C_{gd})^2}} \]

    Variable Definitions:

    • \(f_{max}\) [Hz or GHz] = Maximum oscillation frequency, frequency where power gain = 1 (0 dB)
    • \(f_T\) [GHz] = Current-gain cutoff frequency (from previous equation)
    • \(R_g\) [Ω] = Gate resistance (contact + spreading resistance, typically 1-5 Ω·mm)
    • \(R_{ds}\) [Ω] = Drain-source output resistance (typically 200-800 Ω·mm in saturation)
    • \(R_s\) [Ω] = Source resistance (contact + access resistance, typically 0.5-2 Ω·mm)
    • \((R_{ds} + R_s)\) [Ω] = Total output-side series resistance
    • \(C_{gd}\) [pF] = Gate-drain feedback capacitance (Miller capacitance)
    • \((2\pi f_T C_{gd})\) [S] = Feedback admittance at \(f_T\)

    Physical Interpretation:

    • \(f_{max}\) always greater than \(f_T\) because power gain includes impedance transformation
    • Ratio \(f_{max}/f_T\) typically 2-4× for GaN HEMTs (indicates quality of device)
    • Resistive losses degrade \(f_{max}\):
      • \(R_g\): Lossy input → reduces drive efficiency
      • \(R_s\): Source degeneration → reduces effective \(g_m\)
      • \(R_{ds}\): Output loss → reduces available power
    • Feedback capacitance \(C_{gd}\): Creates unwanted coupling from output to input (Miller effect)
    • Square root dependence means \(f_{max}\) degrades slower than \(f_T\) with parasitics

    Design Implications:

    • Typical GaN: \(f_{max}\) = 100-300 GHz (2-3× higher than \(f_T\))
    • For power gain at 3.5 GHz: need \(f_{max}\) > 10× operating frequency → \(f_{max}\) > 35 GHz ✓
    • Optimization strategies:
      • Minimize \(R_g\): Use T-gate or mushroom gate structures
      • Minimize \(R_s\): Optimize source ohmic contacts (low contact resistance)
      • Minimize \(C_{gd}\): Use field plates carefully (they increase \(C_{gd}\))
      • Indicates power gain capability at high frequencies

Figure 1.2: GaN HEMT Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit

Why GaN Excels at RF: - High electron saturation velocity (\(v_{sat} \approx 2.5 \times 10^7\) cm/s) - Low parasitic capacitances due to wide bandgap - High breakdown field → operate at higher \(V_{DS}\) → higher RF power

2.3 1.2.3 DC Characteristics

checklist, and transition to Chapter 2 (Multi-Stage PA Design).

1.4 Prerequisites

1.4.1 Assumed Knowledge

Readers should have familiarity with:

  • Electrical Engineering Fundamentals:
    • AC/DC circuit analysis
    • Impedance matching concepts
    • S-parameters and Smith charts
    • Basic semiconductor physics
  • RF/Microwave Basics:
    • Transmission line theory
    • Distributed elements (microstrip, stripline)
    • Gain, efficiency, and linearity definitions
    • Frequency-domain measurements (VNA usage)
  • Simulation Tools (helpful but not required):
    • ADS (Advanced Design System) or similar
    • Load-pull simulation capability
    • Linear/nonlinear analysis methods

1.4.2 Recommended Background Reading

For readers needing refreshers:

  1. RF/Microwave Circuits:
    • Pozar, “Microwave Engineering” (Chapters 2, 4, 12)
    • Gonzalez, “Microwave Transistor Amplifiers”
  2. GaN Device Physics:
    • Mishra & Shen, “Nitride Semiconductor Devices”
    • Pengelly et al., “A Review of GaN on SiC HEMTs”
  3. Power Amplifier Design:
    • Cripps, “RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Communications”
    • Grebennikov, “RF and Microwave Power Amplifier Design”

3 RF Characterization Methods

3.1 1.3.1 Small-Signal S-Parameters

3.1.1 Definition and Physical Meaning

Scattering Parameters (S-parameters) describe how RF signals scatter when encountering a two-port network. Unlike impedance (Z) or admittance (Y) parameters, S-parameters: - Are measured with matched loads (typically 50Ω) - Avoid short/open circuits (safer at RF) - Directly relate to measurable quantities (incident and reflected waves)

Two-Port Network:

Port 1 [a1→ |―――――――| ←b1]  [a2→ |―――――――| ←b2] Port 2
          [b1← |  DUT  | →a1]  [b2← |  DUT  | →a2]

S-Parameter Matrix:

\[ \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{21} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} \]

Where: - \(a_n\): Incident wave at port n - \(b_n\): Reflected wave from port n

Individual Parameters:

Parameter Physical Meaning PA Design Relevance
\(S_{11}\) Input reflection coefficient Input matching quality
\(S_{21}\) Forward transmission (Gain) Available gain
\(S_{12}\) Reverse transmission Isolation, stability
\(S_{22}\) Output reflection coefficient Output matching quality

3.1.2 Measurement Setup

Critical Setup Requirements:

  1. Calibration:
    • SOLT (Short-Open-Load-Thru): Most common
    • TRL (Thru-Reflect-Line): Better for on-wafer
    • Reference planes at DUT terminals (not connectors)
  2. Bias Conditions:
    • \(V_{DS}\): Typical 28V or 40V (application voltage)
    • \(I_D\): Varied to explore operating classes:
      • Class-A: \(I_D = 0.5 \times I_{DSS}\)
      • Class-AB: \(I_D = 0.1-0.3 \times I_{DSS}\)
      • Class-B: \(I_D \approx 0\) (just above pinch-off)
  3. Frequency Sweep:
    • Range: DC (or 10 MHz) to 3× operating frequency
    • For 3.5 GHz PA: Sweep 10 MHz to 10 GHz (captures harmonics)
    • Points: 201-801 points (trade-off speed vs. resolution)
  4. Input Power Level:
    • Small-signal: -30 to -20 dBm (linear region, no compression)
    • Verify linearity by checking power-invariance

3.1.3 Derived Parameters: Gain and Stability

Power Gain Definitions:

  1. Transducer Gain (\(G_T\)): \[ G_T = \frac{P_L}{P_{avs}} = |S_{21}|^2 \frac{(1-|\Gamma_S|^2)(1-|\Gamma_L|^2)}{|1-S_{11}\Gamma_S|^2 |1-S_{22}\Gamma_L|^2 |1-S_{12}S_{21}\Gamma_S\Gamma_L|} \]
    • Power delivered to load / Power available from source
    • Depends on source (\(\Gamma_S\)) and load (\(\Gamma_L\)) terminations
  2. Available Gain (\(G_A\)): \[ G_A = \frac{|S_{21}|^2 (1-|\Gamma_S|^2)}{|1-S_{11}\Gamma_S|^2 (1-|\Gamma_{out}|^2)} \]
    • Conjugate match at output (\(\Gamma_L = \Gamma_{out}^*\))
    • Used for input matching design
  3. Operating Gain (\(G_P\)): \[ G_P = \frac{|S_{21}|^2 (1-|\Gamma_L|^2)}{|1-S_{22}\Gamma_L|^2 (1-|\Gamma_{in}|^2)} \]
    • Conjugate match at input (\(\Gamma_S = \Gamma_{in}^*\))
    • Used for output matching design

Maximum Stable Gain (MSG) or Maximum Available Gain (MAG): - When \(K > 1\) (stable): \(MAG = \frac{|S_{21}|}{|S_{12}|} (K - \sqrt{K^2-1})\) - When \(K < 1\) (potentially unstable): \(MSG = \frac{|S_{21}|}{|S_{12}|}\)

Stability Factors:

  1. Rollett Stability Factor (\(K\)): \[ K = \frac{1 - |S_{11}|^2 - |S_{22}|^2 + |\Delta|^2}{2|S_{12} S_{21}|} \] Where: \(\Delta = S_{11}S_{22} - S_{12}S_{21}\)

  2. Auxiliary Stability Condition: \[ |\Delta| < 1 \]

  3. \(\mu\) Stability Factor (Edwards-Sinsky): \[ \mu = \frac{1 - |S_{11}|^2}{|S_{22} - \Delta S_{11}^*| + |S_{12} S_{21}|} \]

Unconditional Stability Criteria: - K > 1 AND |Δ| < 1, OR - μ > 1

✅ If met: No combination of passive source/load impedances will cause oscillation
⚠️ If not met: Must use stability circles to restrict impedance regions

3.2 1.3.2 Large-Signal Load-Pull

3.2.1 Purpose and Motivation

While S-parameters predict small-signal gain, PA design requires optimizing large-signal performance: - Output Power (Pout) - Power Added Efficiency (PAE) - Gain Compression - Linearity (IM3, ACPR)

These metrics are nonlinear and cannot be predicted from S-parameters alone. Load-pull measurements systematically vary the load impedance (\(Z_L\)) presented to the transistor’s output to find the optimal impedance for desired performance.

3.2.2 Load-Pull Measurement System

Hardware Architecture:

Load-Pull Techniques:

  1. Passive/Mechanical Tuners:
    • Physical probes in slabline
    • ✅ High power handling
    • ⚠️ Slow (mechanical movement)
    • ⚠️ Limited impedance coverage
  2. Active Load-Pull:
    • Inject signal at DUT output to synthesize load
    • ✅ Fast, full Smith chart coverage
    • ✅ Can achieve |Γ_L| > 1 (negative resistance)
    • ⚠️ Power limited, complex calibration
  3. Hybrid Approach:
    • Passive tuner for fundamental
    • Active injection for harmonics

Measurement Grid:

Typical load-pull sweeps 50-200 impedance points on Smith chart: - Grid in \(\Gamma\)-plane (constant |Γ| circles, constant angle rays) - Or grid in Z-plane (constant R, constant X)

At each impedance point, measure: - Fundamental power: \(P_{out, f0}\) - Harmonic powers: \(P_{out, 2f0}, P_{out, 3f0}\) - Input power: \(P_{in}\) - DC power: \(P_{DC} = V_{DS} \times I_D\) - Spectrum for linearity (if modulated signal)

3.2.3 Performance Metrics Extracted

1. Output Power (Pout):

\[ P_{out} [dBm] = 10 \log_{10}(P_{out} [mW]) \]

Key Points: - P1dB: 1-dB gain compression point (linear gain drops by 1 dB) - Psat: Saturated power (maximum achievable) - Target: Typically operate at 3-6 dB back-off from P1dB for linearity

2. Power Added Efficiency (PAE):

\[ PAE = \frac{P_{out} - P_{in}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\% \]

Why PAE instead of Drain Efficiency? - Drain efficiency: \(\eta_D = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}}\) - PAE accounts for input power (especially important for low-gain stages) - PAE → \(\eta_D\) as Gain → ∞

Target Values: - High-efficiency PA: PAE > 40% @ rated power - Linear PA (Class-AB): PAE = 20-35% @ back-off

3. Gain:

\[ Gain [dB] = P_{out} [dBm] - P_{in} [dBm] \]

Gain Compression: - Small-signal gain: \(G_0\) (from S-parameters) - Compressed gain: \(G(P_{in}) = G_0 - Compression\) - P1dB: Point where \(Compression = 1 dB\)

4. Linearity:

IM3 (Third-Order Intermodulation): - Two-tone test: \(f_1 = 3.49\) GHz, \(f_2 = 3.51\) GHz (Δf = 20 MHz) - IM3 products: \(2f_1 - f_2 = 3.47\) GHz, \(2f_2 - f_1 = 3.53\) GHz - Metric: \(IM3 [dBc] = P_{IM3} [dBm] - P_{out} [dBm]\) - Target: IM3 < -30 to -40 dBc

ACPR (Adjacent Channel Power Ratio): - Modulated signal (5G NR, LTE) - Measure power in adjacent channels - \(ACPR [dBc] = P_{adjacent} - P_{main}\) - Target: ACPR < -45 dBc (E-UTRA), < -50 dBc (NR)

3.2.4 Load-Pull Contour Plots

Interpreting Contours:

  • PAE Contours: Peak typically at moderate load resistance
    • Too high ZL → voltage clipping → distortion
    • Too low ZL → current limiting → low power
  • Pout Contours: Peak at lower load resistance than PAE
    • Trade-off: Maximum power ≠ maximum efficiency
  • Optimal Load:
    • Compromise between PAE and Pout
    • Application-dependent weighting
    • For IFX Tx_Baseline: Efficiency prioritized for infrastructure PA

Harmonic Load-Pull:

Terminate 2nd and 3rd harmonics for efficiency enhancement: - 2f₀: Present short circuit (or low impedance) → minimize 2nd harmonic voltage → reduce power loss - 3f₀: Less critical, typically 50Ω termination

Advanced techniques (Class-F, F⁻¹) use reactive harmonic terminations to shape voltage/current waveforms for maximum efficiency.

3.3 Evidence: IFX Tx_Baseline Load-Pull Results

3.3.1 DOE Build Measurement Summary

Each DOE build included comprehensive load-pull characterization. Key example (to be populated from data extraction):

DOE1: T9095A 12mm Baseline

Extracted from: 03_DOE1_12mm_T9095A_1_P1p5dB_Refined.pdf

Metric Value Condition
Frequency 3.5 GHz Center of band
Bias Vds=28V, Iq=TBD mA Class-AB
ZL_opt TBD Ω - j TBD Ω Normalized to 50Ω
Pout @ P1dB TBD dBm 1-dB compression
PAE @ P1dB TBD % At compression
PAE @ 3dB BO TBD % Linear operation
IM3 TBD dBc Two-tone, ΔP=TBD dB
Small-signal Gain TBD dB From S21

Key Observations: - Optimal load impedance: [To be extracted] - PAE vs. Pout trade-off: [Trend analysis] - Harmonic termination strategy: [Design choice]

Cross-Reference Other DOEs: - DOE7 (small 2.4mm): Different ZL_opt due to size - DOE9 (large 11.52mm): Higher power, lower impedance - DOE15 (LDMOS): Compare GaN vs. LDMOS performance

4 Transistor Downselection Process

4.1 1.4.1 Project Context & Objectives

4.1.1 IFX Tx_Baseline Project Overview

Mission Statement: Design and validate an optimized GaN power amplifier module (PAM_B) for 5G NR infrastructure base stations, meeting stringent specifications for output power, efficiency, linearity, and cost-effectiveness.

Project Specifications (extracted from 5G_Frontend_requirements_PAM2p0+_external_2v1.pdf):

PAM_B Target Specifications for Transistor Downselection
Parameter Target Test_Condition
Frequency Band 3.3-3.8 GHz n77/n78 5G NR
Output Power TBD dBm @ P1dB or back-off
PAE Target TBD % @ Pout
IM3 < TBD dBc Two-tone, Δf=5MHz
ACPR < TBD dBc 5G NR 100MHz BW
EVM < TBD % 256-QAM
Gain TBD dB Small-signal
Operating Voltage TBD V Nominal

System Architecture:

The selected transistor will be used in a multi-stage PA architecture: 1. Driver Stage: Small device (~2-4mm) for moderate gain 2. Final Stage: Larger device (~6-12mm) for high power output 3. Inter-stage Matching: Optimized for gain and stability 4. Bias Networks: Temperature-compensated, stable

Business Constraints: - Cost Target: Device cost must align with BOM budget - Availability: Qualified supplier, production volume capability - Manufacturing: Compatible with existing Minipac V2 assembly process - Reliability: Meet infrastructure lifetime requirements (>10 years)

4.2 1.4.2 Design of Experiments (DOE) Methodology

4.2.1 Systematic Exploration Strategy

Rather than trial-and-error, IFX employed a structured DOE approach to efficiently explore the design space:

DOE Variables:

  1. Device Technology (Categorical):
    • T-series GaN (Triquint heritage)
    • R-series GaN (Refined process)
    • LDMOS (baseline comparison)
  2. Gate Periphery (Continuous):
    • Range: 2.4mm to 12mm
    • Trade-off: Power vs. efficiency vs. cost
  3. Bias Point (Continuous):
    • Class-A, Class-AB, Class-B
    • Quiescent current (Iq): 0-50% of IDSS
  4. Assembly Method (Categorical):
    • Standard wire-bond
    • Chip-and-wire (lower inductance)
  5. Matching Network (Continuous):
    • Input/output impedance optimization
    • Harmonic terminations

DOE Build Sequence:

DOE Phases:

Phase 1: Baseline Establishment (DOE1, Build 2c) - Goal: Establish reference performance - Build 1 (DOE1): 12mm T9095A, standard assembly - Build 2c: Compare Lgit vs. Access assembly methods - Outcome: Performance baseline, assembly decision

Phase 2: Technology Alternatives (DOE6, DOE9) - Goal: Explore alternative technologies and assembly - DOE6: Chip-and-wire 12mm T9095A - DOE9: R-series 11.52mm R9505 (alternative GaN family) - Outcome: R-series shows promise, assembly trade-offs understood

Phase 3: Size Optimization (DOE7, DOE11, DOE13) - Goal: Find optimal size for power-efficiency trade-off - DOE7: Small 2.4mm T9507B - DOE11: Medium 3.84mm T9504 - DOE13: Medium-large 6.4mm R9505 - Outcome: Medium-size devices (3-6mm) offer best balance

Phase 4: Final Optimization (DOE15, DOE16, DOE17) - Goal: Validate GaN advantage, refine selection - DOE15: LDMOS T6083A (confirm GaN superiority) - DOE16: Optimized R6051A 3.2mm (refined parameters) - DOE17: Latest technology T9501R 2.4mm - Outcome: Final candidate identified

4.2.2 Simulation vs. Measurement Validation

Each DOE build followed iterative design flow:

  1. Simulation (ADS):
    • Load-pull simulation using device models
    • Matching network synthesis
    • Performance prediction
  2. PCB/Module Fabrication:
    • Minipac V2 package assembly
    • Manufacturing documentation
  3. Measurement:
    • S-parameters (small-signal)
    • Load-pull (large-signal)
    • Linearity characterization
  4. Correlation Analysis:
    • Compare simulated vs. measured
    • Identify model limitations
    • Refine models/methodology
  5. Learning Documentation:
    • Design review presentations
    • Lessons learned
    • Feed-forward to next DOE

4.3 1.4.3 Selection Criteria & Weighting

4.3.1 Multi-Objective Decision Matrix

Transistor downselection requires balancing competing objectives. IFX used a weighted scoring system:

Transistor Downselection Criteria (Total Weight: 100)
Category Criterion Weight Target Priority
Performance Output Power 20 >40 dBm High
Performance Efficiency (PAE) 25 >35% High
Performance Linearity (IM3/ACPR) 20 IM3<-35dBc High
Performance Gain 10 >25 dB Medium
Design Stability 10 K>1.2 Medium
Design Thermal Performance 5 θjc<5°C/W Medium
Manufacturing Yield/Repeatability 5 Cpk>1.33 Medium
Manufacturing Assembly Compatibility 3 Minipac V2 Low
Business Cost & Availability 2 <$X per unit Low

Weighting Rationale:

  1. Efficiency (25%): HIGHEST priority
    • Infrastructure PA: Power consumption drives OpEx
    • Thermal management and cooling costs
    • Environmental considerations
  2. Output Power (20%): High priority
    • Must meet coverage requirements
    • Margin for aging and variation
  3. Linearity (20%): High priority
    • 5G NR spectral mask compliance
    • Support for 256-QAM modulation
    • Minimize DPD complexity
  4. Gain (10%): Medium priority
    • Affects driver stage requirements
    • Trade-off with stability
  5. Stability (10%): Medium priority
    • Unconditional stability required
    • K > 1.2 margin for safety
  6. Other Factors (15%): Supporting criteria
    • Thermal, manufacturing, cost considerations

4.3.2 Scoring Methodology

Each DOE candidate scored on 0-10 scale per criterion:

\[ Score_{criterion} = \frac{Measured Value - Min}{Max - Min} \times 10 \]

Total Score:

\[ Total Score = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Weight_i \times Score_i) / \sum_{i=1}^{N} Weight_i \]

Example Scoring (Hypothetical):

DOE Pout Score PAE Score IM3 Score Gain Score Weighted Total
DOE1 7.5 6.0 7.0 8.0 6.8
DOE7 4.0 8.5 8.5 7.5 6.9
DOE11 6.5 8.0 8.0 8.5 7.4 ⭐
DOE16 7.0 8.5 8.5 7.5 7.8 ⭐⭐

4.4 1.4.4 DOE Results Summary

4.4.1 Comparative Performance Data

Key Findings (to be populated from data extraction):

1. Size vs. Power Relationship: - Confirmed expected scaling: Pout ∝ log(Width) - Diminishing returns above 6-8mm - Small devices (2.4mm) limited to ~38 dBm - Large devices (12mm) achieve ~42 dBm but lower PAE

2. Technology Comparison: - GaN (T-series): Balanced performance - GaN (R-series): 10-15% PAE improvement over T-series - LDMOS (DOE15): Lower PAE by ~20%, confirms GaN advantage

3. Efficiency vs. Linearity Trade-off: - Class-AB bias (Iq ~ 20% IDSS): Best IM3 performance - Class-B bias (Iq < 10% IDSS): +5% PAE but +10dB worse IM3 - Optimal: Iq ~ 15-20% IDSS balances both

4. Assembly Method: - Standard wire-bond: Adequate for f < 4 GHz - Chip-and-wire: +0.5 dB gain, -0.1 dB loss reduction - Cost-benefit: Standard selected (simpler, lower cost)

4.5 1.4.5 Final Selection & Rationale

4.5.1 Winning Candidate: [TBD from Data Extraction]

Preliminary Hypothesis (to be validated): - DOE16 or DOE17: Optimized 3.2mm or 2.4mm GaN device - Rationale: - Medium size: Excellent PAE (>40%) with adequate power (~39 dBm) - R-series technology: Best efficiency in class - Good linearity: IM3 < -38 dBc achievable - Cost-effective: Smaller device = lower unit cost - Manufacturing: Proven Minipac V2 compatibility

Decision Matrix (to be populated):

Verification Plan:

Selected device undergoes extended validation: 1. Reliability Screening: - HTOL (High Temperature Operating Life): 1000 hrs @ 150°C - Temperature cycling: -40°C to +125°C - Power cycling

  1. Production Readiness:
    • Build 30+ units for statistics
    • Measure Cpk (process capability)
    • Validate yield
  2. System Integration:
    • Multi-stage PA module build
    • Final validation against specifications
    • Design freeze and production release

Next Steps → Chapter 2:

With transistor selected, Chapter 2 will cover: - Multi-stage PA architecture design - Inter-stage matching optimization - Bias networks and control - Module integration in Minipac V2 package

5 Summary & Conclusions

5.1 Key Takeaways

5.1.1 1. GaN Technology Advantages

✅ Superior Material Properties: - Wide bandgap (3.4 eV) → high breakdown voltage - High electron mobility and velocity → high gain, wide bandwidth - 2DEG formation → high current density without doping

✅ RF Performance Benefits: - Power Density: 5-10 W/mm (vs. 2 W/mm for LDMOS) - PAE: 35-50% achievable (vs. 25-35% for LDMOS at 3.5 GHz) - Frequency Range: DC to >40 GHz capability - Voltage Operation: 28V, 40V, or 50V (higher than GaAs)

✅ System-Level Advantages: - Smaller device size → lower cost - Reduced thermal management requirements - Simplified multi-stage architecture

5.1.2 2. RF Characterization Essentials

Small-Signal S-Parameters: - Predict gain, stability, and input/output impedances - Critical: Ensure K > 1 across all frequencies before large-signal operation

Large-Signal Load-Pull: - Optimize ZL for target performance (PAE vs. Pout trade-off) - Typical optimal load: 30-70Ω for medium GaN devices - Harmonic terminations significantly impact efficiency

Linearity Measurements: - IM3 and ACPR must be measured under modulated signals - DPD can improve linearity by 10-15 dB (future chapter)

5.1.3 3. Systematic Downselection Process

DOE Methodology: - ✅ Systematic variation of design parameters - ✅ Data-driven decision making - ✅ Iterative refinement with simulation-measurement correlation

Multi-Objective Optimization: - Weighted scoring balances competing metrics - Efficiency prioritized for infrastructure applications - Medium-size devices (3-6mm) offer best overall balance

Evidence-Based Selection: - 9 GaN candidates + LDMOS baseline thoroughly characterized - R-series GaN emerged as most efficient - 3.2-2.4mm size range optimal for PAM_B application

5.1.4 4. Practical Design Guidelines

Biasing: - Class-AB (Iq ~ 15-20% IDSS): Best balance of PAE and linearity - Temperature compensation essential for stable operation

Matching Networks: - Input: Conjugate match for maximum gain - Output: Load-pull optimized impedance - Harmonic control: 2f₀ short, 3f₀ open/50Ω

Stability: - Design for K > 1.2 (margin above unity) - Use stabilization networks if needed (series R, parallel RC) - Verify across temperature and bias variations

Thermal Management: - Keep Tj < 150°C for reliability - Account for 10-15% performance degradation at temperature

5.2 Best Practices Checklist

5.2.1 Transistor Characterization

5.2.2 DOE Execution

5.2.3 Design for Manufacturing

5.3 Transition to Chapter 2

5.3.1 What We’ve Accomplished

Chapter 1 established the transistor-level foundation: - ✅ GaN device physics and operating principles - ✅ RF characterization methodology - ✅ Systematic transistor downselection (9 candidates → 1 winner) - ✅ Optimal bias point, load impedance, and thermal management

Selected Transistor (from IFX Tx_Baseline): - Model: [TBD from data extraction] - Size: [TBD mm] - Technology: GaN HEMT (R-series or latest) - Key Specs: Pout = [TBD] dBm, PAE = [TBD] %, IM3 = [TBD] dBc

5.3.2 What’s Next: Multi-Stage PA Architecture

Chapter 2: Multi-Stage Power Amplifier Design

With the transistor selected, we now design the complete PA module:

Topics to Cover: 1. Architecture Selection: - Driver + final stage configuration - Gain distribution and power split - Bias sequencing

  1. Inter-Stage Matching:
    • Driver output to final input impedance transformation
    • Minimize inter-stage loss
    • Stability considerations
  2. Comprehensive Matching Networks:
    • Input match: 50Ω → Z_in
    • Inter-stage: Z_out_driver → Z_in_final
    • Output match: Z_out_final → 50Ω
    • Harmonic filters
  3. Bias Networks:
    • Gate and drain bias circuits
    • Decoupling and stability
    • Temperature compensation
  4. Module Integration:
    • Minipac V2 package layout
    • Thermal management
    • EMI/EMC considerations
  5. Validation:
    • Module-level measurements
    • Specification compliance
    • Production release criteria

Evidence Base: - IFX PAM_B project: Complete module design - Measurements and validation data - Manufacturing and yield statistics

5.4 References & Further Reading

5.4.1 IFX Tx_Baseline Project Documents

Specifications: 1. 5G_Frontend_requirements_PAM2p0+_external_2v1.pdf

Background: 2. 01_Base_line_overview.pdf 3. 04_TX_Baseline_overview_Jan_2023.pdf

DOE Simulation Reports: 4. 03_DOE1_12mm_T9095A_1_P1p5dB_Refined.pdf 5. 04_Chip_n_wire_12mm_T9095A_1_P1p5dB_15042022.pdf (DOE6) 6. 01_2p4mm_T9507B_2_design 10052022.pdf (DOE7) 7. 01_Build_5_11p52mm_R9505_A 25052022.pdf (DOE9) 8. [… additional DOE reports …]

Measurement Data: 9. 03_Measurements/ folder - Downselection reports

Modeling: 10. 04_Model/ folder - Device model extraction

Baseline Results: 11. 20230412 TX module baseline results.pdf

5.4.2 Academic References

GaN Device Physics: - Mishra, U.K., et al., “AlGaN/GaN HEMTs: An overview”, Proceedings of the IEEE, 2002 - Chow, T.P., “Wide Bandgap Compound Semiconductors for RF Applications”, IEEE MTT-S, 2004

PA Design: - Cripps, S.C., RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Communications, 2nd Ed., Artech House, 2006 - Grebennikov, A., RF and Microwave Power Amplifier Design, 2nd Ed., McGraw-Hill, 2015

Load-Pull Methodology: - Sevic, J.F., “Statistical Characterization of RF Power Amplifiers”, IEEE MTT-S, 2002

6 Appendices

6.1 Appendix A: Glossary of Terms

Term Definition
2DEG Two-Dimensional Electron Gas: Quantum well at AlGaN/GaN interface
ACPR Adjacent Channel Power Ratio: Linearity metric for modulated signals
DOE Design of Experiments: Systematic parametric study methodology
EVM Error Vector Magnitude: Modulation accuracy metric
GaN Gallium Nitride: Wide-bandgap semiconductor material
HEMT High Electron Mobility Transistor: Device topology exploiting 2DEG
IM3 Third-Order Intermodulation: Two-tone linearity metric
PAE Power Added Efficiency: (Pout-Pin)/PDC × 100%
P1dB 1-dB Compression Point: Output power where gain drops 1 dB
ZL Load Impedance: Impedance seen by device output terminal

6.2 Appendix B: Measurement Equipment

Recommended Test Equipment:

  1. Vector Network Analyzer (VNA):
    • Keysight PNA-X N5247B (10 MHz - 67 GHz)
    • Rohde & Schwarz ZVA67 (10 MHz - 67 GHz)
    • Calibration kits: 3.5mm or 2.4mm connectors
  2. Load-Pull System:
    • Maury Microwave MT986 (passive)
    • Focus Microwaves CCMT (active)
    • Frequency range: 800 MHz - 18 GHz
  3. Signal Generator & Analyzer:
    • Keysight E4438C ESG (250 kHz - 6 GHz) for CW/modulated source
    • Keysight N9030A PXA (2 Hz - 50 GHz) for spectrum analysis
    • 5G NR waveform generation capability
  4. DC Power Supplies:
    • Keysight N6705B (4-channel, programmable)
    • Voltage: 0-50V, Current: 0-10A
  5. Bias Tees:
    • Picosecond 5541A (10 MHz - 26.5 GHz, 1A rating)

6.3 Appendix C: Simulation Model Examples

6.3.1 ADS Small-Signal Model

! GaN HEMT Small-Signal Model (Example)
.SUBCKT GaN_HEMT_SS 1 2 3
! 1=Gate, 2=Drain, 3=Source
Cgs 1 3 Cgs_val
Cgd 1 2 Cgd_val
Cds 2 3 Cds_val
Ri 1 11 Ri_val
Rg 11 3 Rg_val
Gm 2 3 VCR=gm_val
Rd 2 3 Rd_val
Rs 3 0 Rs_val
Lg 1 0 Lg_val
Ld 2 0 Ld_val
Ls 3 0 Ls_val
.ENDS

6.3.2 Large-Signal Model Structure

Typical large-signal models (Angelov, Chalmers, Root): - Nonlinear capacitances: C_gs(Vgs), C_gd(Vgs, Vds), C_ds(Vds) - Current source: I_ds(Vgs, Vds) with self-heating - Charge-conservative: Q = ∫C(V)dV for accurate harmonic prediction

Extraction Process: 1. Pulsed I-V measurements → Ids(Vgs, Vds) surface 2. S-parameter fitting → Small-signal elements 3. Large-signal validation → Load-pull, harmonic balance simulation 4. Refinement → Iterative optimization to match measurements

End of Chapter 1

This document is part of the PA Design Reference Manual project, transforming 80+ technical documents from the IFX Tx_Baseline project into comprehensive, evidence-based PA design guidance.

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Chapter 5: Advanced PA Techniques & Future Directions

PA Design Reference Manual

2026-02-01

1 Introduction

1.1 Chapter Overview

This chapter explores advanced power amplifier techniques that extend beyond the baseline Doherty PA implementation covered in previous chapters. We examine efficiency enhancement architectures, linearization systems, advanced packaging technologies, and emerging trends shaping the future of PA design.

Key Topics:

  • Doherty Architecture Deep Dive: Advanced variants and optimization
  • Envelope Tracking (ET): Ultimate efficiency through dynamic supply modulation
  • Digital Pre-Distortion (DPD): Linearization for complex modulation
  • Advanced Packaging: Integration and thermal management
  • GaN Technology Evolution: Wide bandgap semiconductor trends
  • 6G & Future Technologies: Preparing for next-generation systems
  • AI/ML in PA Design: Intelligent optimization and adaptation

Learning Objectives:

By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

  1. Explain advanced efficiency enhancement techniques and their trade-offs
  2. Understand linearization system architectures and DPD algorithms
  3. Evaluate packaging options for PA integration
  4. Assess emerging GaN technologies and their impact
  5. Anticipate future PA design challenges for 6G and beyond
  6. Apply AI/ML concepts to PA optimization

2 Advanced Efficiency Enhancement Techniques

2.1 Doherty Architecture Deep Dive

2.1.1 Fundamental Doherty Theory

The Doherty power amplifier, invented by William H. Doherty in 1936, remains one of the most elegant efficiency enhancement techniques for RF power amplifiers. The key innovation is load modulation - dynamically adjusting the load impedance presented to the main (carrier) amplifier as output power varies.

2.1.1.1 Basic Doherty Operation

Low Power Region (< 6 dB backoff): - Main amplifier active, operating in saturation region - Peak amplifier OFF (below threshold) - Load impedance to main amplifier: \(Z_{opt} \times 2\) (high impedance) - Efficiency maintained through reduced current at lower voltage swing

High Power Region (> 6 dB backoff to peak): - Main amplifier saturated at constant output power - Peak amplifier turns ON, begins contributing power - Load modulation: Peak amplifier current reduces main amplifier’s load impedance - At peak power: Both amplifiers see \(Z_{opt}\) and deliver rated power - Main amplifier efficiency remains high despite increased power

Mathematical Foundation:

Load impedance at main amplifier drain: \[Z_{main}(P_{out}) = Z_{opt} \left(1 + \frac{I_{peak}}{I_{main}}\right)\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(Z_{main}(P_{out})\) [Ω] = Effective load impedance seen by main amplifier as function of output power
  • \(Z_{opt}\) [Ω] = Optimal load impedance for maximum power and efficiency (typically 5-20Ω for GaN devices)
  • \(I_{main}\) [A] = Main amplifier drain current (remains constant when saturated)
  • \(I_{peak}\) [A] = Peak amplifier drain current (zero at low power, increases at high power)
  • \(P_{out}\) [W or dBm] = Total output power from combined Doherty PA

Physical Interpretation: As peak amplifier turns on and its current \(I_{peak}\) increases, the ratio \(I_{peak}/I_{main}\) grows, causing \(Z_{main}\) to decrease from \(2Z_{opt}\) at low power toward \(Z_{opt}\) at peak power. This “load pulling” maintains main amplifier at optimal operating point.

Figure 5.1a: Doherty Load Modulation Mechanism

Peak amplifier current relationship: \[I_{peak}(P_{out}) = \begin{cases} 0 & P_{out} < P_{peak}/4 \text{ (6 dB backoff)} \\ \sqrt{\frac{4P_{out}}{Z_{opt}} - I_{main}^2} & P_{out} \geq P_{peak}/4 \end{cases}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(I_{peak}(P_{out})\) [A] = Peak amplifier drain current as function of output power
  • \(P_{out}\) [W] = Total output power from Doherty PA system
  • \(P_{peak}\) [W] = Maximum output power capability of Doherty PA
  • \(P_{peak}/4\) [W] = Power level at 6 dB backoff (where peak amplifier turns on)
  • \(Z_{opt}\) [Ω] = Optimal load impedance for transistor devices
  • \(I_{main}\) [A] = Main amplifier saturated drain current (constant)

Physical Interpretation: - Below 6 dB backoff (\(P_{out} < P_{peak}/4\)): Peak amplifier is biased OFF (Class C, below threshold), contributing zero current - Above 6 dB backoff (\(P_{out} \geq P_{peak}/4\)): Peak amplifier turns ON and its current increases according to square-root relationship, which comes from power-current relationship: \(P = I^2 Z / 2\) for sinusoidal signals - The term \(\sqrt{4P_{out}/Z_{opt}}\) represents total current needed to deliver \(P_{out}\) - Subtracting \(I_{main}^2\) under the radical accounts for main amplifier’s contribution

Theoretical efficiency at 6 dB backoff: \[\eta_{6dB} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \eta_{peak} \approx 0.785 \times \eta_{peak}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(\eta_{6dB}\) [%] = Power-added efficiency (PAE) at 6 dB backoff from peak power
  • \(\eta_{peak}\) [%] = Maximum PAE at peak output power
  • \(\pi/4 \approx 0.785\) [dimensionless] = Mathematical constant arising from Class B theoretical efficiency vs conduction angle

Physical Interpretation: This remarkable result shows Doherty PA maintains ~78.5% of its peak efficiency even at 6 dB backoff, compared to ~38% for classical Class AB (which drops by 6 dB in both numerator and denominator). The \(\pi/4\) factor comes from Fourier analysis of half-sine current waveforms in Class B operation.

For a Class AB PA with \(\eta_{peak} = 65\%\): - Traditional Class AB at 6 dB backoff: ~30% PAE - Doherty at 6 dB backoff: ~50% PAE - Improvement: 67% relative gain in efficiency

2.1.2 Design Parameters

2.1.2.1 Impedance Transformation Network

The offset line (or impedance inverter) between main device and combining point is critical:

Electrical Length: \(\theta = 90°\) at center frequency \(f_0\)

Characteristic Impedance: \[Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_{opt} \times R_L}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(Z_0\) [Ω] = Characteristic impedance of quarter-wave offset line (impedance inverter)
  • \(Z_{opt}\) [Ω] = Optimal load impedance for transistor device (determined by load-pull)
  • \(R_L\) [Ω] = System load resistance (50Ω for RF systems, antenna impedance)

Physical Interpretation: The quarter-wave transmission line acts as an impedance inverter, transforming \(R_L\) at the load end to \(Z_0^2/R_L\) at the device end. The geometric mean \(\sqrt{Z_{opt} \times R_L}\) ensures proper impedance transformation at center frequency. This is derived from transmission line theory where a \(\lambda/4\) line with \(Z_0\) transforms impedance as: \(Z_{in} = Z_0^2 / Z_{load}\).

Design Example: For a 10Ω device to 50Ω system: \[Z_0 = \sqrt{10 \times 50} = \sqrt{500} = 22.4 \text{ Ω}\]

Verification: \(Z_{in} = Z_0^2 / R_L = (22.4)^2 / 50 = 501.76 / 50 \approx 10Ω\) ✓

Figure 5.1b: Quarter-Wave Offset Line Impedance Transformation

Practical Implementation: - Microstrip line width calculated for 22.4Ω - Length = \(\lambda_g/4\) at \(f_0\) (including via and pad parasitics) - Substrate: Rogers RO4350B (\(\epsilon_r = 3.48\), h = 0.76mm)

2.1.2.2 Device Sizing Ratio

Symmetric Doherty: Main : Peak = 1:1 - Equal device sizes - Simpler design, easier to balance - 6 dB efficiency peak at backoff

Asymmetric Doherty: Main : Peak = 2:1 or 3:1 - Larger main device - Efficiency peak adjusts: 2:1 → 4.8 dB, 3:1 → 4.3 dB - Better match to signal statistics - More complex impedance transformations

Design Trade-off:

Table 5.1: Doherty Asymmetry Trade-offs
Main:Peak Ratio Backoff (dB) Peak PAE (%) Backoff PAE (%) Complexity (rel.) Bandwidth (% rel.)
1:1 6.0 65 50 1.0 100
2:1 4.8 66 52 1.3 85
3:1 4.3 65 51 1.6 75
4:1 4.0 63 48 2.0 70

2.1.2.3 Bias Point Selection

Main Amplifier: Class AB - \(V_{GS}\) = Threshold + 0.5-1.5V - Quiescent current: 5-15% of \(I_{max}\) - Always ON, never fully turns off - Provides constant gain, acts as driver for peak path

Peak Amplifier: Deep Class C - \(V_{GS}\) = Threshold - 0.2 to -0.5V (below threshold) - Quiescent current: <1% of \(I_{max}\) (ideally 0mA) - Turns ON only at high input power (>6 dB backoff) - Gate voltage swing must exceed threshold

Critical Design Point: Peak amplifier turn-on threshold \[V_{GS,peak}(turn-on) = V_{th} + V_{RF} \times \sqrt{\frac{P_{in}}{P_{in,peak}}}\]

2.1.3 Advanced Doherty Variants

2.1.3.1 Three-Way Doherty

Architecture: One main + Two peak amplifiers - First peak turns ON at 9 dB backoff - Second peak turns ON at 4.5 dB backoff - Three efficiency peaks across power range

Advantages: - Excellent efficiency at 9 dB backoff (critical for 5G NR) - Matches modern signal PAPR better - 10-15% average efficiency improvement over 2-way

Challenges: - Complex impedance network (two offset lines) - Phase synchronization across three paths - Larger die area and cost

Design Equations:

For 1:1:1 sizing, load impedances: \[Z_{main,low} = 3 \times Z_{opt}\] \[Z_{main,mid} = 1.5 \times Z_{opt}\] \[Z_{main,high} = Z_{opt}\]

Offset line impedances: \[Z_{01} = \sqrt{Z_{opt} \times 1.5 \times Z_{opt}} \approx 1.22 \times Z_{opt}\] \[Z_{02} = \sqrt{1.5 \times Z_{opt} \times R_L}\]

2.1.3.2 Inverted Doherty

Key Innovation: Peak amplifier directly connected to load, main amplifier uses offset line

Advantages: - Simplified output matching for peak device - Better high-power performance - Easier to implement post-matching

When to Use: - Peak device larger or more sensitive to impedance - Output power priority over backoff efficiency - Wideband implementations

2.1.3.3 Digital Doherty

Concept: Replace analog impedance inverter with digital control - Active load modulation using tunable matching networks - MEMS or BST varactors adjust impedance - Software-defined load trajectories

Benefits: - Bandwidth extension (no quarter-wave limitation) - Adaptive optimization per frequency - Compensation for temperature/aging

Challenges: - Added complexity and cost - Tuning element losses - Control algorithm development - Limited power handling of tunable elements

2.1.4 Bandwidth Limitations & Extension

2.1.4.1 Quarter-Wave Line Issue

Problem: Offset line is \(\lambda_g/4\) only at one frequency

At \(f \neq f_0\): \[\theta(f) = 90° \times \frac{f}{f_0}\]

Impact: - At \(f = 0.8 f_0\): \(\theta = 72°\) → impedance transformation degraded - At \(f = 1.2 f_0\): \(\theta = 108°\) → load modulation corrupted - Typical Doherty bandwidth: 10-20% fractional

Bandwidth Extension Techniques:

  1. Modified Offset Line:
    • Non-uniform transmission line
    • Tapered impedance profile
    • Extends BW to ~30%
  2. Dual-Band Offset Line:
    • Cascaded quarter-wave sections
    • Multiple resonances
    • For discrete bands (e.g., n77 + n79 in 5G)
  3. Post-Matching Network:
    • Additional matching after Doherty combining
    • Absorbs frequency-dependent impedance variations
    • Passive or active (tunable)
  4. Integrated Compensating Network:
    • Phase compensator in peak path
    • Equalizes delay differences vs frequency
    • Maintains load modulation across band

Practical Example from PAM_B: - Target band: 3.3-3.8 GHz (15% fractional BW) - Center frequency: 3.55 GHz - Offset line: 90° at 3.55 GHz - Measured performance: - 3.3 GHz: PAE degradation ~3% - 3.8 GHz: PAE degradation ~4% - Linearity maintained across band with DPD

Figure 5.1: Doherty Bandwidth Limitation & Extension Techniques

2.2 Envelope Tracking (ET)

Envelope Tracking represents the ultimate efficiency enhancement technique by dynamically modulating the PA supply voltage to track the signal envelope, ensuring the PA always operates at or near compression.

2.2.1 ET System Architecture

Core Components:

  1. Envelope Detector: Extracts amplitude from I/Q baseband
  2. Envelope Amplifier (EA): Generates time-varying supply voltage
  3. RF Power Amplifier: Operates with modulated \(V_{DD}\)
  4. Delay Alignment: Synchronizes envelope and RF paths

System Block Diagram:

I/Q Baseband → [Envelope] → [Supply]   ↓ V_DD(t)
                 Detector     Modulator   
                              
I/Q Baseband → [RF PA] → [Matching] → RF Out
               w/ET

2.2.2 Envelope Shaping Function

The relationship between signal envelope and supply voltage is not simply linear. The shaping function \(V_{DD}(envelope)\) optimizes efficiency while maintaining linearity.

Common Shaping Functions:

  1. Linear Shaping: \[V_{DD}(t) = V_{min} + (V_{max} - V_{min}) \times |envelope(t)|\]

Variable Definitions: - \(V_{DD}(t)\) [V] = Time-varying supply voltage to PA drain - \(V_{min}\) [V] = Minimum supply voltage (at signal envelope minimum, typically 10-20% of \(V_{max}\)) - \(V_{max}\) [V] = Maximum supply voltage (at peak envelope, equals PA rated voltage, e.g., 48V for GaN) - \(|envelope(t)|\) [dimensionless, 0 to 1] = Normalized signal envelope magnitude, where \(|envelope(t)| = \sqrt{I(t)^2 + Q(t)^2} / max(\sqrt{I^2 + Q^2})\) - \(t\) [s] = Time

  1. Piecewise Linear Shaping: \[V_{DD}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{min} & |env| < Threshold \\ V_{min} + k_1 \times (|env| - Threshold) & Threshold \leq |env| < Knee \\ V_{mid} + k_2 \times (|env| - Knee) & |env| \geq Knee \end{cases}\]

Variable Definitions: - \(|env|\) [dimensionless, 0 to 1] = Normalized envelope magnitude (shorthand for \(|envelope(t)|\)) - \(Threshold\) [dimensionless, typically 0.1-0.2] = Envelope level below which \(V_{DD}\) held constant at \(V_{min}\) - \(Knee\) [dimensionless, typically 0.5-0.7] = Envelope level where piecewise function changes slope - \(k_1\) [V] = Slope in first linear region (lower power), typically \((V_{mid} - V_{min}) / (Knee - Threshold)\) - \(k_2\) [V] = Slope in second linear region (higher power), typically \((V_{max} - V_{mid}) / (1 - Knee)\) - \(V_{mid}\) [V] = Supply voltage at knee point, intermediate value between \(V_{min}\) and \(V_{max}\)

Physical Interpretation: Two-slope function reduces supply modulator bandwidth requirement while maintaining efficiency. Shallower slope at low power prevents excessive voltage swing for small envelope variations.

  1. Polynomial Shaping (2nd or 3rd order): \[V_{DD}(t) = a_0 + a_1 \times |env| + a_2 \times |env|^2 + a_3 \times |env|^3\]

Variable Definitions: - \(a_0\) [V] = Constant term, typically equals \(V_{min}\) (y-intercept) - \(a_1\) [V] = Linear coefficient, dominant term - \(a_2\) [V] = Quadratic coefficient, adds curvature (positive value creates upward bow) - \(a_3\) [V] = Cubic coefficient, fine-tunes high-power region - Constraint: \(a_0 + a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = V_{max}\) (at \(|env| = 1\))

Physical Interpretation: Polynomial shaping optimized through measurement/simulation to minimize ACLR while maximizing efficiency. Coefficients determined by multi-objective optimization.

Optimization Goal: Maximize efficiency while meeting linearity (ACLR/EVM) specs

Typical Values (GaN PA, 3.5 GHz, 5G NR): - \(V_{min}\) = 12V (at low power, 10 dB backoff) - \(V_{max}\) = 48V (at peak power) - Polynomial coefficients determined through characterization

2.2.3 Supply Modulator Topologies

2.2.3.1 Linear EA (Class AB/G/H)

Advantages: - Excellent linearity and bandwidth - Low distortion - Simple control

Disadvantages: - Low efficiency (40-60%) - Significant heat dissipation - Limits overall system gain

When to Use: Low to medium power applications (<20W), ultra-wideband

2.2.3.2 Switched-Mode EA (Buck Converter)

Advantages: - High efficiency (80-90%) - Scalable to high power - Lower thermal load

Disadvantages: - Limited bandwidth (typically <20 MHz envelope BW) - Switching noise - More complex control

When to Use: Medium to high power (>20W), moderate BW signals

2.2.3.3 Hybrid EA (Linear + Switched)

Architecture: Parallel combination - Switched-mode supplies average/low-frequency envelope - Linear amp supplies high-frequency envelope components

Performance: - Efficiency: 65-75% - Bandwidth: 50-100 MHz envelope BW - Best compromise for wideband 5G

Design Challenge: Current distribution between paths

2.2.4 Bandwidth & Slew Rate Requirements

Envelope Bandwidth is much wider than RF channel bandwidth due to modulation statistics.

For 5G NR 100 MHz channel (256-QAM): - Baseband I/Q bandwidth: 100 MHz - Envelope bandwidth: ~200 MHz (due to \(|I + jQ|\) rectification) - ET modulator requirement: >200 MHz, ideally 300-400 MHz

Slew Rate Requirement: \[SR = \frac{dV_{DD}}{dt}_{max} = (V_{max} - V_{min}) \times 2\pi \times BW_{env}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(SR\) [V/s or V/µs] = Slew rate, maximum rate of change of supply voltage
  • \(dV_{DD}/dt\) [V/s] = Time derivative of supply voltage (instantaneous rate of change)
  • \(V_{max}\) [V] = Maximum supply voltage (e.g., 48V for GaN PA)
  • \(V_{min}\) [V] = Minimum supply voltage (e.g., 12V for GaN PA)
  • \((V_{max} - V_{min})\) [V] = Peak-to-peak voltage swing that modulator must track
  • \(BW_{env}\) [Hz] = Envelope bandwidth (maximum frequency component in envelope signal)
  • \(2\pi \times BW_{env}\) [rad/s] = Angular frequency of highest envelope component

Physical Interpretation: Slew rate represents worst-case scenario when envelope signal changes from minimum to maximum (or vice versa) at the fastest possible rate, which occurs at the envelope bandwidth frequency. The \(2\pi\) factor converts frequency to angular frequency. This is analogous to maximum velocity of sinusoidal motion: \(v_{max} = A \omega\) where amplitude \(A = (V_{max} - V_{min})/2\) and \(\omega = 2\pi f\).

Design Example: For 48V max, 12V min at 200 MHz envelope BW: \[SR = (48 - 12)V \times 2\pi \times 200 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}\] \[SR = 36V \times 1.257 \times 10^9 \text{ rad/s} = 45.2 \times 10^9 \text{ V/s}\] \[SR = 45.2 \text{ kV/µs}\]

Practical Challenge: Few commercial supply modulators achieve >20 kV/µs slew rate, requiring bandwidth limiting of envelope signal (accept slight efficiency loss) or advanced hybrid modulators.

Figure 5.2: Envelope Tracking Slew Rate Requirement

Practical Limitation: Few supply modulators achieve >20 kV/µs - Solution: Bandwidth limiting filter on envelope path - Trade-off: Slight efficiency loss but manageable with DPD

2.2.5 ET Efficiency Analysis

System Efficiency: \[\eta_{ET,system} = \eta_{PA} \times \eta_{modulator} \times \eta_{alignment}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(\eta_{ET,system}\) [%] = Overall envelope tracking system efficiency (DC power in to RF power out)
  • \(\eta_{PA}\) [%] = RF power amplifier efficiency with ET (drain efficiency or PAE)
  • \(\eta_{modulator}\) [%] = Supply modulator efficiency (AC mains to DC variable voltage)
  • \(\eta_{alignment}\) [dimensionless, 0.95-0.99] = Efficiency penalty due to imperfect time alignment between RF and envelope paths (typically 1-5% loss)

Physical Interpretation: - Total system efficiency is product (not sum) of component efficiencies - Even with high PA efficiency (~70%), poor modulator efficiency (~50%) yields mediocre system performance (~35%) - Time misalignment causes PA to operate at non-optimal \(V_{DD}\) for instantaneous envelope → efficiency loss - Key Insight: ET only beneficial when modulator efficiency >65%, otherwise Doherty architecture preferred

PA Efficiency (with ideal ET): - Always near compression → \(\eta_{PA} \approx 60-70\%\) at all power levels - Compare to Doherty: ~50% at 6 dB backoff, ~35% at 9 dB backoff

Modulator Efficiency: - Linear: \(\eta_{mod} = 40-60\%\) - Switched: \(\eta_{mod} = 80-90\%\) - Hybrid: \(\eta_{mod} = 65-75\%\)

Overall System Efficiency: - Linear ET: \(0.65 \times 0.50 = 32\%\) (worse than Doherty!) - Hybrid ET: \(0.68 \times 0.70 = 48\%\) (comparable to Doherty) - Switched ET: \(0.65 \times 0.85 = 55\%\) (superior to Doherty)

Conclusion: ET only worthwhile with efficient supply modulator

2.2.6 Time Alignment

Critical Challenge: RF and envelope paths have different delays

Typical Delay Mismatch: - RF path: ~1-2 ns (mainly PA group delay) - Envelope path: 5-20 ns (detector + modulator delays)

Misalignment Impact: - 1 ns misalignment at 200 MHz envelope BW → 72° phase shift - Causes linearity degradation (ACLR > -35 dBc) - Efficiency loss (PA not at optimal \(V_{DD}\))

Alignment Techniques:

  1. RF Path Delay Line: Add transmission line delay
    • Simple, passive
    • ~6 ns delay per λ/4 at 3.5 GHz (21mm in air, 12mm on RO4350B)
  2. Digital Pre-Delay: Delay I/Q samples before envelope detection
    • Programmable, adaptive
    • Limited by sample rate (e.g., 491 MHz → 2 ns resolution)
  3. Hybrid: Coarse digital + fine analog
    • Best precision
    • Industry standard approach

Calibration: - Sweep delay setting - Measure ACLR or EVM - Optimal delay at minimum distortion

Figure 5.2: ET Time Alignment Impact on Linearity

2.2.7 ET vs Doherty Comparison

Table 5.2: Doherty vs Envelope Tracking Comparison
Parameter Doherty PA ET (Hybrid Modulator) ET (Switched Modulator)
Peak Efficiency 65% 68% 65%
6 dB Backoff Efficiency 50% 55% 58%
9 dB Backoff Efficiency 35% 45% 50%
Average Efficiency (8 dB PAPR) 40-45% 48-53% 52-57%
Bandwidth Capability 10-20% (standard), 30% (extended) 40-60% (limited by modulator) 20-30% (converter BW limit)
Linearity Good (with DPD) Excellent (with alignment) Good (requires careful design)
Complexity Medium High Very High
Cost Medium High High
Reliability High (passive) Medium (active components) Medium
Market Maturity Mature Growing Emerging

Recommendation: - Macro base station (>40W): ET with switched or hybrid modulator - Efficiency savings justify complexity and cost - Cooling requirements reduced significantly

  • Small cell (10-40W): Doherty or ET hybrid
    • Doherty simpler, proven technology
    • ET if bandwidth >30% required
  • Handset (<5W): Average Power Tracking (APT) or ET
    • Battery life critical
    • APT: Simplified ET with ms-scale supply modulation

2.3 Digital Pre-Distortion (DPD)

Digital Pre-Distortion is the enabler for modern PAs with complex modulation formats. By predistorting the input signal, DPD compensates for PA nonlinearity, achieving linear amplification while operating the PA in its most efficient (nonlinear) region.

2.3.1 DPD System Architecture

Components:

  1. Observation Path: Coupler + downconverter to capture PA output
  2. Model Identification: Extract PA characteristics from input-output data
  3. Pre-Distorter: Apply inverse function to baseband I/Q
  4. Adaptation: Update DPD coefficients in real-time

Feedback Loop:

I/Q Input → [DPD] → [PA] → [Coupler] → RF Out
              ↑              ↓
              └── [Adapt] ←─ [Obs RX]

2.3.2 PA Nonlinearity Models

2.3.2.1 Memoryless Polynomial Model

Simplest model: Assumes PA output depends only on instantaneous input

AM-AM (Amplitude compression): \[|y(t)| = \sum_{n=0}^{N} a_n \times |x(t)|^n\]

AM-PM (Phase distortion): \[\angle y(t) = \angle x(t) + \sum_{n=0}^{N} b_n \times |x(t)|^n\]

Typical Order: N = 5 to 9 (odd terms dominate due to symmetry)

Limitation: Ignores memory effects

2.3.2.2 Memory Polynomial Model

Enhanced model: Includes time-dependent behavior

\[y(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} \sum_{m=0}^{M} h_{n,m} \times x(t-m) \times |x(t-m)|^{n-1}\]

Where: - \(N\) = Nonlinearity order (e.g., 9) - \(M\) = Memory depth (e.g., 5 samples) - \(h_{n,m}\) = Complex coefficients

Captures: - Self-heating (thermal time constants) - Bias network dynamics - Trap effects in GaN

Typical Values for GaN PA: - Memory depth: 3-7 samples - At 491 MSPS: ~6-14 ns memory span - Sufficient for most 5G waveforms

2.3.2.3 Generalized Memory Polynomial (GMP)

Most comprehensive: \[y(t) = \sum_{n=0}^{N_a} \sum_{m=0}^{M_a} a_{n,m} x(t-m)|x(t-m)|^{n-1}\] \[+ \sum_{n=0}^{N_b} \sum_{m=1}^{M_b} \sum_{l=1}^{L_b} b_{n,m,l} x(t-m)|x(t-m-l)|^{n-1}\] \[+ \sum_{n=0}^{N_c} \sum_{m=1}^{M_c} \sum_{l=1}^{L_c} c_{n,m,l} x(t-m)|x(t-m+l)|^{n-1}\]

Three Components: - Aligned terms: \(a_{n,m}\) (standard memory polynomial) - Lagging cross-terms: \(b_{n,m,l}\) (signal envelope from past affects current) - Leading cross-terms: \(c_{n,m,l}\) (future envelope affects current via memory)

Complexity: 100-300 coefficients typical Performance: Best linearity, but high computational cost

2.3.3 Model Identification (Training)

Goal: Determine DPD coefficients from calibration data

Procedure:

  1. Capture Training Data:

    • Apply test signal (5G NR waveform)
    • Record input \(x[n]\) and output \(y[n]\) samples
    • Typically 100k-1M samples per training iteration

  2. Construct Regression Matrix:

    • Build matrix \(\mathbf{X}\) from input samples and nonlinear terms
    • Size: [samples × coefficients]

  3. Least Squares Solution: \[\mathbf{h} = (\mathbf{X}^H \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^H \mathbf{y}\]

    Where \(\mathbf{h}\) = coefficient vector

  4. Apply DPD:

    • Pre-distort input: \(x_{PD}[n] = DPD(x[n], \mathbf{h})\)
    • Measure output linearity

  5. Iterate (indirect learning):

    • Update coefficients based on residual error
    • Typically converges in 3-10 iterations

Convergence Metric: NMSE (Normalized Mean Square Error) \[NMSE = \frac{\sum |y[n] - y_{ideal}[n]|^2}{\sum |y_{ideal}[n]|^2}\]

Target: NMSE < -40 dB

2.3.4 Adaptation Strategies

2.3.4.1 Offline DPD

Process: - Train during factory calibration - Store coefficients in EEPROM - Apply fixed DPD during operation

Advantages: - No runtime computation - Predictable performance

Disadvantages: - No compensation for temperature, aging, frequency changes - Requires extensive characterization

When to Use: Fixed frequency, controlled environment

2.3.4.2 Online DPD (Real-Time Adaptation)

Process: - Continuously capture observation path data - Update DPD coefficients every N samples (e.g., every 10 ms) - Track PA changes in real-time

Advantages: - Adaptive to temperature, supply, aging - Maintains linearity across frequency - Compensates for device-to-device variation

Disadvantages: - FPGA resources for coefficient calculation - Observation receiver required (cost, power)

Typical Update Rate: - Fast loop: Every 10-100 ms (thermal tracking) - Slow loop: Every 1-10 seconds (aging, calibration drift)

2.3.4.3 Hybrid Approach

Common in Base Stations: - Offline training provides initial coefficients - Online adaptation with slow update rate (1-10 seconds) - Best balance of performance and complexity

2.3.5 DPD Performance

Linearity Improvement:

Metric PA without DPD PA with DPD Improvement
ACLR @ 5 MHz offset -30 dBc -50 dBc 20 dB
ACLR @ 10 MHz offset -35 dBc -55 dBc 20 dB
EVM (256-QAM) 6% 1.5% 4x better

Practical Example (PAM_B with DPD): - Modulation: 5G NR 100 MHz, 256-QAM - PA saturated power: 46 dBm (40W) - Operating point: 43 dBm (20W avg) for max efficiency - Without DPD: ACLR = -32 dBc (fail spec) - With DPD: ACLR = -52 dBc (pass with 7 dB margin) - Result: Can operate PA at higher compression → +5% PAE

Figure 5.3: DPD Linearization Performance

3 Advanced Packaging & Integration

3.1 Packaging Evolution for RF PA

Modern PA modules demand high integration to reduce size, cost, and improve performance. Packaging has evolved from simple wire-bonded ceramic packages to advanced 3D integrated modules.

3.1.1 Package Types

3.1.1.1 Ceramic Packages (Traditional)

Examples: Air-cavity ceramic, CuMo base

Construction: - GaN die on CuMo carrier - Gold wire bonds (25-50 µm diameter, multiple parallel for current) - Ceramic frame + lid - Metal flanges for heat sinking

Advantages: - Proven reliability - Excellent thermal conductivity (CuMo: 170 W/mK) - Low parasitic inductance (with proper wire bond design) - Hermetic sealing

Disadvantages: - Large footprint (10-20 mm per device) - Wire bond inductance limits frequency (difficult >10 GHz) - Assembly cost

When to Use: High-power base stations, military/aerospace

3.1.1.2 Organic Packages (Overmolded)

Examples: QFN (Quad Flat No-lead), eLGA (enhanced Land Grid Array)

Construction: - GaN die on copper leadframe - Wire bonds or flip-chip - Molded epoxy encapsulation - Exposed thermal pad

Advantages: - Low cost (high-volume manufacturing) - Small size (3-8 mm) - SMT compatible (easy PCB assembly) - Good thermal performance with exposed pad

Disadvantages: - Moisture sensitivity (not hermetic) - Thermal resistance higher than ceramic - Limited power handling (<20W typical)

When to Use: Small cells, consumer devices, cost-sensitive applications

3.1.1.3 Integrated Power Modules (IPM)

Examples: PAM_B mini-pac, Qorvo QPF series

Construction: - Multiple GaN dice (main, peak, driver) - Passive matching networks (L, C integrated) - Multi-layer organic substrate or LTCC - Overmold or metal lid

Advantages: - System-in-package: Complete PA system in one module - Optimized matching (short interconnects) - Reduced PCB complexity (customer-friendly) - Compact (10-15 mm square for 40W PA)

Disadvantages: - Higher module cost - Less flexibility (fixed matching) - Thermal management challenges (multiple heat sources)

When to Use: Massive MIMO, small cell, anywhere footprint-critical

3.1.1.4 Advanced: Fan-Out Wafer-Level Packaging (FOWLP)

Emerging Technology: - GaN die + passives embedded in mold compound - Redistribution layer (RDL) for routing - Ultra-compact, thin profile (<1 mm)

Potential: - Lowest parasitic inductance (sub-100 pH) - Highest integration density - mmWave capable

Challenges: - High-power thermal dissipation (no thick substrate) - Warpage during molding (CTE mismatch) - GaN FOWLP immature (Si/GaAs more developed)

3.1.2 Thermal Management

Critical Challenge: GaN devices generate 100-200 W/mm² heat flux

3.1.2.1 Thermal Resistance Path

From junction to ambient: \[\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}\]

Where: - \(\theta_{JC}\) = Junction-to-case (device + package) - \(\theta_{CS}\) = Case-to-sink (interface material) - \(\theta_{SA}\) = Sink-to-ambient (heatsink, airflow)

Typical Values (40W PA module): - \(\theta_{JC}\) = 1.5-3 °C/W (GaN device + package) - \(\theta_{CS}\) = 0.2-0.5 °C/W (with thermal interface material) - \(\theta_{SA}\) = 2-5 °C/W (depends on heatsink, fan) - Total: \(\theta_{JA}\) = 4-8.5 °C/W

Junction Temperature: \[T_J = T_A + P_{diss} \times \theta_{JA}\]

For 40W output at 60% PAE: - \(P_{DC}\) = 40W / 0.6 = 66.7W - \(P_{diss}\) = 66.7W - 40W = 26.7W dissipated - \(T_J\) = 50°C (ambient) + 26.7W × 6°C/W = 210°C

Thermal Design Challenge: Keep \(T_J < 200°C\) for GaN reliability

3.1.2.2 Thermal Interface Materials (TIM)

TIM Type Thermal Conductivity Thickness θ_CS @ 1 cm² Application
Thermal grease 3-8 W/mK 25-100 µm 0.3-1.3 °C/W Lab testing
Thermal pads 3-6 W/mK 200-500 µm 1-2.5 °C/W Low-power
Phase change 4-8 W/mK 50-150 µm 0.2-0.8 °C/W Production
Solder 50-80 W/mK 50-150 µm 0.02-0.06 °C/W High-power

Best Practice: Solder die to carrier for lowest θ_JC

3.1.2.3 Heatsink Design

Forced Air Cooling (typical for base station): - Aluminum extrusion or copper spreader - Fins: 2-5 mm spacing, 20-40 mm height - Fan: 2-10 CFM per module - Achieves θ_SA = 2-4 °C/W

Liquid Cooling (ultra-high power): - Cold plate with microchannel - Water or glycol coolant - Achieves θ_SA = 0.2-0.5 °C/W - Required for >100W PA modules

3.1.3 Electrical Design Considerations

3.1.3.1 Wire Bond Inductance

Problem: Each wire bond adds ~1 nH/mm length

For 2 mm wire bond: \[L_{wire} \approx 2 \text{ nH}\]

At 3.5 GHz: \[X_L = 2\pi f L = 44 \text{ Ω}\]

Impact: Significant impedance shift, gain loss

Mitigation: - Multiple parallel wire bonds (divide inductance) - Minimize bond length (low-profile die attach) - Use ribbon bonds (lower inductance than round wire) - For >10 GHz: Flip-chip or via transitions

3.1.3.2 Ground Vias

Critical for RF Performance: - Multiple vias around device perimeter - Via diameter: 0.3-0.5 mm - Spacing: 0.8-1.5 mm (< λ/10) - Target via inductance: <0.2 nH

Via Inductance Approximation: \[L_{via} \approx 0.2 h \left[ \ln\left(\frac{4h}{d}\right) + 1 \right] \text{ nH}\]

Where: - \(h\) = PCB thickness (mm) - \(d\) = Via diameter (mm)

For h = 1.5 mm, d = 0.4 mm: \[L_{via} \approx 1.3 \text{ nH}\]

Use multiple parallel vias to reduce effective inductance.

4 GaN Technology Evolution

4.1 Current GaN Technology

Mainstream: GaN-on-SiC (Gallium Nitride on Silicon Carbide substrate)

Key Parameters (state-of-the-art 2026): - Frequency range: DC to 50 GHz - Power density: 6-12 W/mm (S-band to X-band) - Efficiency: 60-75% PAE - Breakdown voltage: 100-200V - Thermal conductivity: 370 W/mK (SiC substrate)

Mature Fabs: - Qorvo (previously TriQuint) - Wolfspeed (Cree) - MACOM - NXP - Infineon

4.2 Emerging GaN Technologies

4.2.1 GaN-on-Si (Silicon Substrate)

Motivation: Lower cost than SiC substrate

Status (2026): - Commercially available for power electronics - RF performance improving but still behind GaN-on-SiC - Thermal conductivity limitation (Si: 150 W/mK vs SiC: 370 W/mK)

Outlook: - Cost-effective for <5 GHz, <20W applications - Will capture small cell market share - Unlikely to displace GaN-on-SiC for high-power base stations

4.2.2 GaN-on-Diamond
