Ultimate Thermal Solution: - Diamond thermal conductivity: 2000 W/mK (5x better than SiC!) - Enables extreme power density: >20 W/mm

Challenges: - Diamond growth on GaN (lattice mismatch) - Cost (synthetic diamond expensive) - Manufacturing maturity (lab demos only as of 2026)

Potential Applications: - mmWave >28 GHz (Ka-band, V-band) - Ultra-compact phased arrays - Space applications (radiation hard + thermal)

Timeline: Commercial availability 2028-2030 (optimistic)

4.2.3 Vertical GaN

Current GaN Limitation: Lateral devices (source-drain on surface) - High on-resistance - Limited voltage scaling

Vertical GaN (like Si/SiC MOSFETs): - Current flows through substrate thickness - Much lower on-resistance - Higher voltage (>1000V possible) - Better thermal path

Status: Research phase, promising for power electronics

RF Potential: - Ultra-wideband operation (DC-18 GHz demonstrated) - Could revolutionize PA architecture

Timeline: 5-10 years to commercial RF devices

4.3 GaN vs GaAs vs Si LDMOS

Table 5.3: RF Power Amplifier Technology Comparison (2026)
Parameter GaN-on-SiC GaAs Si LDMOS GaN-on-Si
Max Frequency 50 GHz 100 GHz 3.8 GHz 10 GHz
Power Density 10 W/mm 1-2 W/mm 2-4 W/mm 4-6 W/mm
Efficiency 65-75% 45-60% 55-65% 60-70%
Voltage 50-100V 5-12V 28-50V 50-80V
Thermal Conduct. 370 W/mK 46 W/mK 150 W/mK 150 W/mK
Cost High Medium Low Medium-Low
Maturity Mature Very Mature Very Mature Emerging
Key Application 5G macro BS Handsets, mmWave Low-cost BS Small cell

Technology Selection Guide:

  • Frequency < 2 GHz, Cost-sensitive: Si LDMOS
  • 2-6 GHz, High power (>20W): GaN-on-SiC
  • 2-6 GHz, Low power (<10W): GaN-on-Si or GaAs
  • 6-40 GHz, Any power: GaN-on-SiC
  • >40 GHz, <5W: GaAs (for now)
  • >40 GHz, >5W: GaN-on-SiC or GaN-on-diamond (future)

5 6G & Future Technologies

5.1 6G Requirements (2030+ Deployment)

5.1.1 Frequency Bands

Sub-7 GHz: Similar to 5G - FR1: 450 MHz - 7.125 GHz - PA technology: GaN-on-SiC (mature)

mmWave Extended: - FR2: 24.25 - 71 GHz (extended from 5G’s 52.6 GHz) - FR3 (new): 7.125 - 24.25 GHz (mid-band) - PA technology: GaN-on-SiC, GaN-on-diamond

Sub-THz (exploration): - 92-114.25 GHz (W-band) - 130-174.8 GHz (D-band) - PA technology: GaN, InP (Indium Phosphide), possibly graphene

5.1.2 Performance Targets

Metric 5G NR 6G (Target) PA Challenge
Peak Data Rate 20 Gbps 1 Tbps Higher bandwidth → memory effects
PAPR 9-12 dB 10-15 dB Even higher efficiency challenge
Modulation 256-QAM 1024-QAM, 4096-QAM Extreme linearity (EVM <0.5%)
Spectrum Efficiency 30 bps/Hz 100+ bps/Hz Wideband operation
Energy Efficiency 1x 10-100x Average PAE must reach 45-55%

Key PA Challenge for 6G: High efficiency at extreme backoff (12-15 dB)

5.2 Advanced PA Architectures for 6G

5.2.1 Dual-Input Doherty

Innovation: Separate inputs to main and peak amplifiers - Enables different signal processing per path - Asymmetric DPD (different models for main vs peak) - Bandwidth extension through per-path matching

Advantage: 5-10% efficiency gain over conventional Doherty

5.2.2 Load Modulated Balanced Amplifier (LMBA)

Concept: Combines load modulation with balanced amplifier - Four-port network (two inputs, two outputs) - Achieves wideband load modulation without quarter-wave lines - Demonstrated 60% fractional bandwidth

Status: Research → early products expected 2027-2028

Potential: Game-changer for ultra-wideband 6G

5.2.3 Outphasing Revival

Modern Twist: Digital control + adaptive combining - Addresses traditional outphasing issues (combiner loss) - Active load control using tunable matching - Software-defined load trajectories

6G Application: Sub-THz frequencies - Constant envelope → high-efficiency amplifier stages - Linearization through signal processing

5.2.4 Hybrid Analog-Digital Beamforming PA Arrays

Massive MIMO Evolution: - 64-256 antenna elements per panel - PA per element (low power, 0.25-1W each) - Combined beam steering (analog) + precoding (digital)

PA Requirements: - Ultra-compact (3-5 mm spacing) - Moderate linearity (beamforming averages distortion) - High efficiency at low power - Low cost (100s of PAs per base station)

Technology: GaN-on-Si + advanced packaging (FOWLP)

5.3 AI/ML in PA Design

Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning are transforming PA design, optimization, and operation.

5.3.1 AI-Assisted Design Flow

Traditional Design: Manual iteration 1. Set specs → 2. Design matching → 3. Simulate → 4. Fab → 5. Measure → 6. Tune → Repeat

AI-Enhanced Design: 1. Generative Design: AI proposes initial matching networks 2. Multi-Objective Optimization: RL agent explores design space 3. Predictive Simulation: ML model predicts performance (faster than EM) 4. Intelligent Tuning: AI guides CV tuning process

Benefits: - 10x faster design cycle - Discovers non-intuitive solutions - Reduces prototype iterations

5.3.2 ML for DPD

Traditional DPD: Fixed polynomial model

ML-Enhanced DPD: - Neural Network DPD: Feedforward network learns PA inverse function - Architectures: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory), GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) - Captures complex memory effects better than polynomials - Demonstrated 3-5 dB ACLR improvement

  • Reinforcement Learning DPD: Agent learns optimal predistortion policy
    • Adapts to changing PA characteristics
    • Multi-objective optimization (linearity + efficiency)

Challenges: - Training data requirements (10^6 - 10^7 samples) - Real-time inference (latency <1 ms) - FPGA implementation complexity

Status (2026): - Research → early commercial deployments - Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ FPGA enables real-time NN-DPD

5.3.3 Self-Healing PA Systems

Concept: PA module that adapts to failures

ML Role: - Anomaly Detection: Identify performance degradation - Monitors: PAE, linearity, temperature, reflected power - Detects: Component failure, bias drift, thermal issues

  • Adaptive Reconfiguration: Compensate for issues
    • Adjust bias points
    • Modify DPD model
    • Switch to backup paths (redundant PAs)
  • Predictive Maintenance: Forecast failures before they occur
    • Analyze trends in monitored parameters
    • Alert for preventive action

Value Proposition: - Reduced downtime for base stations - Extended PA lifetime - Lower maintenance costs

Deployment: Cloud RAN architecture ideal for centralized ML

5.3.4 Digital Twin for PA

Concept: Virtual replica of physical PA module

Components: - Physics-Based Model: EM + thermal + nonlinear device - Data-Driven Model: ML trained on measurements - Real-Time Sync: Updates from actual PA telemetry

Applications: - “What-If” Analysis: Test scenarios before deployment - Optimization: Find optimal operating point in simulation - Failure Analysis: Replay conditions leading to issue

Implementation: - Cloud-hosted digital twin - Edge device reports telemetry - Twin runs 100-1000x faster than real-time

6 Summary & Key Takeaways

6.1 Efficiency Enhancement Techniques

Doherty PA: - ✅ Proven, mature technology - ✅ 50% PAE at 6 dB backoff (vs 30% for Class AB) - ✅ Moderate complexity and cost - ❌ Limited bandwidth (10-30%) - Best for: Sub-6 GHz macro base stations

Envelope Tracking: - ✅ Superior efficiency (55%+ average PAE possible) - ✅ Wideband capable (40-60% fractional BW) - ❌ High complexity (supply modulator required) - ❌ Time alignment critical - Best for: Wideband systems, high-power (>40W)

Outphasing: - ✅ Constant envelope → simple PAs - ❌ Combiner losses - ❌ Signal decomposition complexity - Best for: Specialized applications, sub-THz

6.2 Linearization

DPD is Essential for modern PAs: - Enables 10-20 dB ACLR improvement - Allows operation closer to compression → higher efficiency - Memory polynomial sufficient for most 5G applications - Real-time adaptation becoming standard

Next Generation: Neural network DPD - Better linearity (3-5 dB ACLR gain) - Adaptive to changing conditions - Requires FPGA/ASIC for inference

6.3 Packaging & Integration

Trend: Higher integration (system-in-package) - Multiple devices + matching in one module - Reduces PCB complexity - Enables compact designs (massive MIMO)

Thermal Management is critical: - GaN power density demands excellent heat sinking - Target: \(T_J < 200°C\) for reliability - Solder attach + forced air typical for base station

6.4 Future Outlook

6G Challenges: - Even higher PAPR (12-15 dB) → efficiency at extreme backoff - 1024-QAM, 4096-QAM → ultra-high linearity (EVM <0.5%) - Sub-THz frequencies → new PA architectures - Energy efficiency 10-100x improvement needed

Enabling Technologies: - GaN-on-diamond (thermal performance) - Vertical GaN (wideband, high voltage) - Advanced packaging (FOWLP for mmWave) - AI/ML for design, optimization, adaptation

AI/ML Impact: - Faster design cycles (generative design) - Better DPD (neural networks) - Self-healing systems (anomaly detection) - Digital twins (virtual testing)

6.5 Recommended Reading

  1. Doherty PA Design:
    • Cripps, S. C., “Advanced Techniques in RF Power Amplifier Design”, Artech House, 2002
    • Raab, F. H., “Efficiency of Doherty RF Power-Amplifier Systems”, IEEE Trans. Broadcasting, 1987
  2. Envelope Tracking:
    • Kim, J., et al., “Wideband Envelope Tracking Power Amplifiers With Reduced Bandwidth Power Supply Waveforms and Adaptive Digital Predistortion Techniques”, IEEE Trans. Microwave Theory Tech., 2009
  3. DPD:
    • Wood, J., “Behavioral Modeling and Linearization of RF Power Amplifiers”, Artech House, 2014
    • Morgan, D. R., et al., “A Generalized Memory Polynomial Model for Digital Predistortion of RF Power Amplifiers”, IEEE Trans. Signal Processing, 2006
  4. GaN Technology:
    • Mishra, U. K., Shen, L., Kazior, T. E., Wu, Y.-F., “GaN-Based RF Power Devices and Amplifiers”, Proc. IEEE, 2008
    • Pengelly, R. S., et al., “A Review of GaN on SiC High Electron-Mobility Power Transistors and MMICs”, IEEE Trans. Microwave Theory Tech., 2012
  5. Future Technologies:
    • Viikari, V., et al., “Beamforming and Enabling Technologies for 6G”, IEEE Communications Standards Magazine, 2024
    • Zhang, H., et al., “Artificial Intelligence for 6G Wireless Systems”, IEEE Wireless Communications, 2025

Chapter 5 Complete
Next: Chapter 6: Lessons Learned & Practical Wisdom →

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Chapter 6: Lessons Learned & Practical Wisdom

PA Design Reference Manual

2026-02-01

1 Introduction

1.1 What Makes This Chapter Different

This chapter distills 12 years of RF power amplifier design experience into actionable wisdom. Unlike previous chapters that focus on theory and methodologies, this chapter addresses the real challenges you’ll face in practice - the ones that textbooks don’t cover.

What You’ll Find Here:

  • War Stories: Real failures (and their fixes) from actual projects
  • Debugging Strategies: Systematic approaches to troubleshooting
  • Design Shortcuts: Techniques that save weeks of iteration
  • Career Guidance: Growing from junior engineer to PA expert
  • Interview Preparation: Deep technical Q&A based on actual interviews

Why This Matters:

“Experience is what you get when you didn’t get what you wanted.”
— Randy Pausch

The goal is to compress years of learning into one chapter, helping you avoid common pitfalls and accelerate your journey to PA design mastery.

2 Common Design Pitfalls & Solutions

2.1 Stability Nightmares

2.1.1 The Problem

Scenario: You’ve designed a beautiful PA. Simulations show 30 dB gain, 65% PAE. You build it, power up, and… oscillation at 1.2 GHz. Or maybe worse - intermittent instability that appears only at certain temperatures or power levels.

Why This Happens:

Power amplifiers are positive-gain devices operating at high power levels. Any unintended feedback path can create oscillation. The challenge: feedback can come from many sources:

  1. Device Internal Feedback (\(C_{gd}\), \(C_{ds}\))
  2. Packaging Parasitics (wire bonds, leadframe)
  3. Layout Coupling (substrate modes, ground loops)
  4. Bias Network Resonances
  5. Harmonic Mixing (2nd harmonic mixing down to low frequency)

2.1.2 Stability Analysis Review

Rollett Stability Factor (K): \[K = \frac{1 - |S_{11}|^2 - |S_{22}|^2 + |\Delta|^2}{2|S_{12}||S_{21}|}\]

\[\Delta = S_{11}S_{22} - S_{12}S_{21}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(K\) [dimensionless] = Rollett stability factor (also called K-factor), measures unconditional stability
  • \(S_{11}\) [dimensionless, complex] = Input reflection coefficient (how much power reflects at input port)
  • \(S_{22}\) [dimensionless, complex] = Output reflection coefficient (how much power reflects at output port)
  • \(S_{12}\) [dimensionless, complex] = Reverse transmission coefficient (feedback from output to input)
  • \(S_{21}\) [dimensionless, complex] = Forward transmission coefficient (gain from input to output)
  • \(|S_{ij}|\) [dimensionless, 0 to 1] = Magnitude of S-parameter
  • \(\Delta\) [dimensionless, complex] = Determinant of S-parameter matrix, measures internal feedback

Physical Interpretation: - \(K > 1\) indicates device can be made stable for any passive load (unconditional stability) - \(K < 1\) means device potentially unstable - oscillation possible with certain load impedances - \(S_{12}\) represents internal feedback (gate-to-drain capacitance \(C_{gd}\) in HEMTs) - Large \(S_{21}\) (high gain) with significant \(S_{12}\) (feedback) → lower K → more prone to oscillation - Frequency dependent: Must check K from DC to 10× operating frequency

Stability Criteria: - \(K > 1\) AND \(|\Delta| < 1\) → Unconditionally stable - \(K < 1\) → Potentially unstable, must check load/source stability circles - Design Target: \(K > 1.5\) for robust stability margin

Figure 6.1: K-Factor Stability Analysis Example

2.1.3 Real-World Example: PAM_B Stability Issue

Symptom: Main amplifier oscillating at 1.8 GHz (below operating band 3.3-3.8 GHz)

Investigation: 1. Spectrum Analyzer: Clean signal at low power, oscillation appears >30 dBm output 2. K-Factor Analysis: K = 0.6 at 1.8 GHz (unstable!) 3. Root Cause: Bias network resonance at 1.8 GHz created low-impedance path - Gate bias: 100 µH inductor + 10 nF cap - Self-resonant frequency: \(f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = 1.59\) GHz - At high power: Large voltage swing excites resonance

Solution: - Added series resistor (100Ω) in gate bias line - Damps resonance (Q reduced from 50 to 5) - No impact on DC bias point - Small RF loss acceptable (only bias network) - Added parallel RC snubber across bias inductor - 47Ω + 47 nF - Absorbs energy at resonance - Result: K-factor > 1.3 from 0.1-10 GHz, oscillation eliminated

Lesson Learned: > “Analyze stability from DC to 10× your operating frequency, not just in-band.”

2.1.4 Stability Design Checklist

Input Stabilization:

  • ✅ Add series resistance (22-100Ω) on gate/base input
    • Cost: -0.5 to -1 dB gain
    • Benefit: Prevents low-frequency instability
  • ✅ Input RC network to ground
    • Typical: 33Ω + 100 pF before device
    • Shunts low-frequency energy
  • ✅ Ferrite bead on bias line (for low-freq < 100 MHz)

Output Stabilization:

  • ✅ Drain/collector series resistance (optional, high-power only)
    • Use only if instability persists after input stabilization
    • Impacts efficiency (-2-5% PAE loss)

Bias Network:

  • ✅ Check self-resonance frequency of L-C networks
    • Should be well below operating frequency (typically <1 GHz)
  • ✅ Damping resistors on bias inductors
    • Parallel: High-value (1-10 kΩ, minimize DC loss)
    • Series: Low-value (22-100Ω, if needed)
  • ✅ Multiple decoupling capacitors
    • Mix values: 10 nF, 100 nF, 1 µF, 10 µF
    • Covers broad frequency range

Layout:

  • ✅ Star grounding for bias
    • Single-point ground prevents ground loops
  • ✅ Minimize loop area
    • Especially input/output coupling
  • ✅ Ground vias: Many, closely spaced
    • Target: <λ/10 at highest frequency of concern

Verification:

  • ✅ Simulate K-factor from 0.1 GHz to 10×f₀
  • ✅ Load-pull stability check (vary load, look for oscillation)
  • ✅ Temperature cycling test (instability often temperature-dependent)
  • ✅ Power sweep (some oscillations only appear at high drive)

2.2 Thermal Runaway

2.2.1 The Catastrophic Failure Mode

What is Thermal Runaway?

A positive feedback loop: 1. Power dissipation heats device 2. Higher temperature increases drain current (\(I_{DS}\) increases) 3. More current → more power dissipation 4. Even higher temperature → runaway → device destruction

GaN vs GaAs Behavior:

  • GaAs: Strong positive temperature coefficient
    • \(I_{DS}\) increases significantly with T
    • Prone to thermal runaway
    • Requires ballasting (source resistance)
  • GaN: Negative or weakly positive temperature coefficient
    • \(I_{DS}\) slightly decreases with T (at low \(V_{DS}\))
    • More thermally stable
    • But NOT immune!

When GaN Still Fails: - Localized hotspots: Non-uniform current distribution - Thermal-electrical interaction: Hot spot draws less current, but surrounding area draws more → other areas fail - Repeated thermal cycling: Crack formation in die attach

2.2.2 Warning Signs

Before Catastrophic Failure:

  1. Increasing Quiescent Current
    • Monitor \(I_{DQ}\) over time

    • 10% increase → thermal degradation likely

  2. Gain Compression Shift
    • P1dB moves to lower power
    • Indicates device heating
  3. Efficiency Degradation
    • PAE drops 5-10% relative → thermal issue
  4. Visible Hot Spots (thermal camera)
    • Non-uniform temperature across die

    • 30°C difference → current crowding

2.2.3 Prevention Strategies

2.2.3.1 Thermal Design Margin

Philosophy: Design for 50% power dissipation margin

Example: - PA delivers 40W at 60% PAE - Power dissipation: \((40W / 0.6) - 40W = 26.7W\) - Design thermal system for: \(26.7W \times 1.5 = 40W\) dissipation - Margin accounts for: Process variation, aging, worst-case conditions

Thermal Resistance Calculation:

\[\theta_{JA} = \frac{T_{J,max} - T_{A,max}}{P_{diss,design}}\]

Variable Definitions:

  • \(\theta_{JA}\) [°C/W] = Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (total thermal resistance from die to air)
  • \(T_{J,max}\) [°C] = Maximum allowable junction temperature (typically 150-200°C for GaN, 150°C for reliability)
  • \(T_{A,max}\) [°C] = Maximum ambient temperature in operating environment (typically 40-70°C for base stations)
  • \(P_{diss,design}\) [W] = Design power dissipation including margin (actual dissipation × safety factor)

Physical Interpretation: - Thermal resistance is analogous to electrical resistance: \(\theta = \Delta T / P\) (like \(R = \Delta V / I\)) - Lower \(\theta_{JA}\) means better heat removal → junction runs cooler - Total thermal resistance is series sum of components:

\[\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}\]

Where: - \(\theta_{JC}\) [°C/W] = Junction-to-case (through die, die attach, package base) - \(\theta_{CS}\) [°C/W] = Case-to-heatsink (through thermal interface material, TIM) - \(\theta_{SA}\) [°C/W] = Heatsink-to-ambient (through fins, convection/radiation)

Design Example: For GaN \(T_{J,max} = 200°C\), \(T_{A,max} = 50°C\): \[\theta_{JA} = \frac{200 - 50}{40} = 3.75 \text{°C/W}\]

This requirement drives heatsink selection. With typical \(\theta_{JC} = 1.5\) °C/W and \(\theta_{CS} = 0.5\) °C/W: \[\theta_{SA,required} = 3.75 - 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.75 \text{°C/W}\]

Figure 6.2: Thermal Resistance Network and Temperature Profile

Practical Considerations: - Forced air cooling: Achieve \(\theta_{SA} \approx 1-3\) °C/W - Natural convection: Achieve \(\theta_{SA} \approx 5-15\) °C/W - Liquid cooling: Achieve \(\theta_{SA} \approx 0.1-0.5\) °C/W

This drives heatsink selection.

2.2.3.2 Thermal Simulation

When to Simulate: - Early design phase: Size die attach, select package - Layout phase: Optimize via placement, copper pours - Verification: Confirm margin before prototyping

Tools: - ANSYS Icepak: 3D thermal FEA, couples to HFSS - Simcenter Flotherm: Specialized for electronics cooling - COMSOL Multiphysics: Full multi-physics (thermal + electrical)

What to Model: - Die power dissipation (from RF simulation) - Package thermal resistance (from datasheet) - PCB stack-up (copper, dielectric, vias) - Heatsink (geometry, airflow) - Ambient conditions (temperature, convection)

Validation: - Thermal camera measurement vs simulation - Thermocouple on package - Target: <10% error

2.2.3.3 Thermal Monitoring

In-Circuit Monitoring: - Thermistor: On PCB near PA, analog output - NTC Thermistor: Negative temperature coefficient - Typical: 10 kΩ @ 25°C - β = 3950 K - Resistance: \(R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}\)

Protection Logic: - Threshold 1 (80°C): Warning, log event - Threshold 2 (100°C): Reduce PA power - Threshold 3 (120°C): Shut down PA

Over-Temperature Protection (OTP): - Implemented in FPGA or microcontroller - Response time: <100 ms - Hysteresis to prevent oscillation

2.2.4 Real Example: Thermal Failure in Lab

Project: High-power Doherty PA (80W)

Symptom: After 10 minutes of operation at max power, output power drops by 3 dB, then PA stops responding.

Investigation: 1. Thermal camera: Junction temperature estimated >250°C (GaN melting point: 290°C!) 2. Post-mortem: Die attach voiding visible under X-ray 3. Root cause: Insufficient die attach coverage (only 60% of die backside)

Why Happened: - Assembly process issue: Solder voids during reflow - No thermal simulation performed (rushed prototype) - No thermal monitoring during test

Solution: - Improved die attach process: Vacuum reflow to eliminate voids - X-ray inspection: 100% coverage required (acceptance criteria) - Thermal simulation: Implemented for all future designs - Thermal shutdown: Added OTP circuit

Result: No thermal failures in subsequent 50 units produced

Lesson Learned: > “Thermal design is not optional. It’s the difference between a working PA and molten silicon.”

2.3 Matching Network Failures

2.3.1 “Why Isn’t My PA Making Power?”

Symptom: PA delivers 10 dB less power than expected. Efficiency terrible. Simulations looked great.

Most Common Root Causes:

  1. Wrong Load Impedance (80% of cases)
  2. Harmonic Impedances Ignored (15% of cases)
  3. Component Parasitics (5% of cases)

2.3.2 Debugging Systematic Approach

2.3.2.1 Step 1: Verify Device is Functional

Quick Checks: - Measure quiescent current (\(I_{DQ}\)) - Should match datasheet ±20% - If zero or >2× expected → device issue

  • Check gate/drain voltages with DMM
    • \(V_{GS}\) should be at bias point
    • \(V_{DS}\) should equal supply voltage
  • Input S11 measurement (with power off)
    • Should be near simulation
    • If very different → device damaged or wrong device

If Device OK → Matching Network Issue

2.3.2.2 Step 2: Characterize Actual Load Impedance

Method 1: Network Analyzer + De-embedding

Measure S-parameters at device reference plane: - Remove PCB, measure looking into output matching - De-embed transmission lines, vias - Compare to load-pull optimal impedance

Method 2: Reproduce Load-Pull

If available, measure PA on load-pull system: - Sweep load impedance on Smith chart - Find impedance that maximizes power (will differ from design!) - This is your actual optimal impedance

Method 3: Time-Domain Reflectometry (TDR)

Identify impedance discontinuities: - 50Ω cable → PA output - TDR shows reflections at each impedance change - Locate faulty component or bad via

Common Findings: - Via inductance not modeled (+0.3 nH typical) - Capacitor parasitic inductance (ESL) - Microstrip width tolerance (±10% → impedance shift) - Unexpected coupling between traces

2.3.2.3 Step 3: Fix or Re-Match

Quick Fixes (if close to target):

  • Tune with variable capacitors: SMD trimmer caps
    • Fine-tune series/shunt capacitance
    • Once optimal value found → replace with fixed cap
  • Add/remove short transmission line stubs
    • Change electrical length
    • Re-optimize

Re-Design (if far from target):

  • Update matching network topology
  • Fabricate new PCB or substrate

2.3.3 Harmonic Impedance Issues

Often Overlooked: PA performance depends on 2nd and 3rd harmonic impedances, not just fundamental!

Why Harmonics Matter:

  • Efficiency: Harmonic termination affects voltage/current waveform shape
    • Class AB optimal: \(Z_{2f0}\) = short, \(Z_{3f0}\) = short (Class B-like)
    • Class E optimal: Specific reactive terminations
  • Linearity: Harmonic distortion products mix with fundamental
    • Poor harmonic termination → worse ACLR

Design Approach:

  1. Load-Pull at Fundamental: Find \(Z_{opt,f0}\)
  2. Harmonic Load-Pull: Fix \(Z_{f0}\), sweep \(Z_{2f0}\) and \(Z_{3f0}\)
    • Find impact on PAE, linearity
    • Often find 2-5% PAE improvement with optimal harmonic termination
  3. Design Matching for Fundamental + Harmonics
    • Challenging: One network must present multiple impedances at different frequencies
    • Tools: Harmonic balance simulation, optimization

Practical Shortcut:

For wideband matching networks, harmonics often naturally terminated close to short circuit (good!). For narrowband, may need explicit harmonic traps.

2.3.4 Component Self-Resonance

The Silent Killer of RF Designs

Every component has parasitic L, C → self-resonant frequency (SRF)

Capacitors: - SRF: Frequency where parasitic inductance resonates with capacitance - Above SRF: Capacitor acts like inductor! - Typical Values: - 100 pF, 0402: SRF ~5 GHz (usable to 3 GHz) - 10 pF, 0402: SRF ~10 GHz (usable to 6 GHz) - 1 nF, 0603: SRF ~1 GHz (usable to 600 MHz)

Inductors: - SRF: Frequency where parasitic capacitance resonates with inductance - Above SRF: Inductor acts like capacitor! - Typical Values: - 10 nH, 0402: SRF ~6 GHz - 100 nH, 0603: SRF ~1.5 GHz

Design Rule: > “Use components with SRF > 2× operating frequency”

Check Component Datasheets: Most RF component vendors (Murata, TDK, Johanson) provide S-parameter models including parasitics.

2.4 When Simulation Lies

2.4.1 The Sim-Meas Gap

Universal Truth: > “Simulation is a guide, not a guarantee.”

Why Simulations Diverge from Measurements:

  1. Model Accuracy (50% of discrepancies)
    • Device models at best ±10% accurate
    • Large-signal models more uncertain than small-signal
    • Temperature effects often not modeled
  2. Parasitic Elements (30%)
    • Wire bonds: ~1 nH/mm
    • Vias: 0.2-0.5 nH each
    • Package leadframe inductance
    • Substrate losses
  3. Assumptions & Simplifications (20%)
    • Ideal components (actual have tolerance, loss)
    • Perfectly uniform PCB (actual has thickness variation)
    • No mutual coupling (actual has EM coupling)

2.4.2 Critical Sim-Meas Differences

Table 6.1: Common Simulation vs Measurement Discrepancies
Parameter Typical Error Primary Cause Mitigation Strategy
Gain ±1 dB Parasitics, device model EM sim, margin
Output Power ±0.5 dB Device model, thermal Conservative design
PAE ±5-10% Thermal effects, loss Thermal simulation
ACLR ±5-10 dB Memory effects, model Behavioral model, DPD
Input Match ±3 dB Via inductance, layout EM co-sim, TDR check
Output Match ±4 dB Parasitics, harmonics Harmonic load-pull
Stability ±0.2-0.5 (K) Layout coupling, bias Broadband analysis

2.4.3 Improving Simulation Accuracy

2.4.3.1 EM Simulation

When to Use: - Any passive structure (matching networks, power dividers) - Transitions (via, bondwire, package) - Any frequency >2 GHz

Tools: - ANSYS HFSS: Most accurate, slow (hours per simulation) - Keysight Momentum: ADS integrated, medium speed - Sonnet: Planar EM, fast for stratified media

Best Practice: - Simulate critical sections, not entire PA - Export S-parameter models, import to circuit simulator - Include: Vias, bondwires, substrate stack-up

2.4.3.2 Electro-Thermal Co-Simulation

Purpose: Couple RF simulation with thermal analysis

Workflow: 1. RF simulation → Power dissipation map 2. Thermal simulation → Junction temperature 3. Update device model with temperature 4. Re-run RF simulation → Iterate

Tools: - ANSYS: HFSS + Icepak coupling - Keysight: ADS + Simcenter Flotherm

Impact: Captures PAE degradation at high power (typically 5-15% lower than isothermal simulation)

2.4.3.3 Conservative Design Margins

Philosophy: Assume simulation is optimistic

Margin Guidelines:

Parameter Simulation Target Actual Spec Margin
Gain Spec + 2 dB Spec 2 dB
Output Power Spec + 1 dB Spec 1 dB
PAE Spec + 10% Spec 10% rel.
ACLR Spec - 5 dB Spec 5 dB
Stability (K) K > 1.5 K > 1.0 0.5

Why This Works: - Accounts for sim-meas gap - Allows for process variation (device-to-device) - Provides tuning headroom - Reduces prototype iterations

Cost: Slightly larger die or component count → acceptable trade-off

2.5 Linearity: The Perennial Challenge

2.5.1 Why ACLR is Never Good Enough

Scenario: Your PA meets power and efficiency specs. But ACLR = -38 dBc (need -45 dBc). EVM = 4% (need <3%). What now?

2.5.2 Root Cause Analysis

2.5.2.1 Beyond AM-AM: The AM-PM Problem

Common Misconception: “I’ll back off power to reduce distortion” - Reality: AM-PM (phase distortion) often dominates linearity at backoff

AM-PM Sources: - Transconductance phase shift vs bias point - Capacitance modulation (\(C_{gs}\), \(C_{gd}\) vary with voltage) - Thermal modulation (temperature affects electrical length)

Measurement: - Two-tone test: Measure IM3 vs power - Spectrum: ACLR vs average power - Vector analyzer: EVM vs input power

If ACLR doesn’t improve with backoff → AM-PM dominant

2.5.2.2 Memory Effects

Definition: PA output depends on past input (not just instantaneous)

Types:

  1. Electrical Memory (short-term, ns scale)
    • Bias network dynamics (not instantaneous supply)
    • Device trap states (GaN has significant trapping)
  2. Thermal Memory (long-term, µs-ms scale)
    • Temperature follows average power
    • Self-heating modulates \(V_{th}\), \(g_m\)

Measurement: - Modulated signal vs CW tone at same average power - Modulated typically shows worse ACLR (memory effects)

Mitigation: - Better bias decoupling (low-impedance at envelope frequencies) - DPD with memory terms (memory polynomial)

2.5.2.3 Driver Stage Nonlinearity

Often Overlooked: Driver amplifier preceding final PA contributes distortion!

Check: - Measure driver output spectrum (before final PA) - If driver already has ACLR = -40 dBc, final PA can’t achieve -50 dBc

Solution: - Linearize driver (DPD, design improvement) - Use higher IP3 driver device - Increase driver power rating (operate at lower compression)

2.5.3 Linearization Techniques

2.5.3.1 Bias Optimization

Simple but Effective: Adjust \(V_{GS}\) (gate bias voltage)

Procedure: 1. Set constant input power (6 dB backoff) 2. Sweep \(V_{GS}\) from threshold to Class A 3. Measure ACLR at each bias point 4. Find “sweet spot” (often between 10-30% \(I_{max}\))

Typical Improvement: 3-8 dB ACLR

Trade-off: May sacrifice 2-5% PAE for linearity

2.5.3.2 Pre-Distortion (Analog)

Concept: Add small nonlinear circuit at input that predistorts signal

Example: Diode predistorter - Schottky diode in series or shunt at input - Creates AM-AM expansion that compensates PA compression - Simple, passive, no power required

Limitation: No memory compensation, fixed (not adaptive)

2.5.3.3 Digital Pre-Distortion (DPD)

Gold Standard: See Chapter 5 for full treatment

Quick Summary: - 10-20 dB ACLR improvement typical - Enables operation closer to saturation → higher efficiency - Requires FPGA, observation receiver - Essential for modern 5G PAs with 256-QAM

3 Design Efficiency & Best Practices

3.1 Requirements Analysis: Do This First!

3.1.1 Understanding the “Why” Behind Specs

Never blindly accept specifications. Understand the system context:

Key Questions:

  1. Why this frequency range?
    • Single band vs multi-band
    • Future-proofing or immediate need
  2. Why this power level?
    • Link budget calculation
    • Margin for cable loss, aging
    • What happens if 1 dB less?
  3. Why this efficiency target?
    • Thermal constraint
    • Power supply limitation
    • Cost (cooling system)
  4. Why this linearity spec?
    • Modulation format drives it
    • Regulatory requirement or system-level
    • Can DPD close gap?

Outcome: - Identify truly critical specs - Negotiate relaxation of over-spec’d parameters - Avoid over-designing (wastes time and money)

3.1.2 The 80/20 Rule in PA Design

Observation: - 80% of effort goes to last 20% of performance - Last 2 dB of power, last 3% of PAE, last 5 dB of ACLR

Strategy: - Deliver 80% performance in 20% of schedule - Prototype early, validate concept - Iterate for final 20% only if needed

Example from PAM_B: - Initial design: 38 dBm, 58% PAE, ACLR -42 dBc (3 weeks) - Final design: 40 dBm, 63% PAE, ACLR -52 dBc (12 more weeks!) - First prototype validated Doherty concept, second optimized details

3.2 Design Flow Best Practices

3.2.1 Systematic Approach

Phase 1: Architecture Selection (Week 1-2) - ✅ Evaluate technology options (GaN vs GaAs, etc.) - ✅ Choose PA class/topology (Class AB, Doherty, ET) - ✅ Select device (based on load-pull data) - ✅ High-level block diagram

Deliverable: Architecture document with rationale

Phase 2: Detailed Design (Week 3-6) - ✅ Device characterization (if not available) - S-parameters, load-pull, large-signal measurements - ✅ Matching network design - Simulate in ADS/Microwave Office - Optimize for multi-objective (power, PAE, BW) - ✅ Bias network design - DC analysis, stability check - ✅ Layout design - EM simulation of critical sections - Thermal simulation

Deliverable: Complete schematic + layout + simulations

Phase 3: Prototype & Test (Week 7-8) - ✅ Fabrication (PCB or substrate) - ✅ Assembly - ✅ Initial RF test (S-parameters, CW power) - ✅ Modulated signal test (ACLR, EVM)

Deliverable: Test data + comparison to simulation

Phase 4: Tuning & Optimization (Week 9-12) - ✅ Component value tuning (if needed) - ✅ Bias optimization - ✅ DPD integration - ✅ Reliability testing (temperature, aging)

Deliverable: Production-ready design + test report

Total: ~12 weeks for new PA design (experienced engineer)

3.2.2 Design Reviews: When and What

Preliminary Design Review (PDR) - After Phase 1 - Attendees: Lead engineer, system engineer, project manager - Topics: Architecture choice, feasibility, risk assessment - Go/No-Go Decision: Proceed to detailed design?

Critical Design Review (CDR) - After Phase 2 - Attendees: Full team + senior engineer review - Topics: Detailed design, simulations, layout - Checks: - All specs can be met (with margin) - Design is manufacturable - BOM cost within target - No single-point failures - Go/No-Go: Proceed to prototype?

Post-Prototype Review - After Phase 3 - Topics: Test results vs simulation, issues encountered - Decision: Tuning only, or re-design needed?

Benefit: Catches issues early, saves rework

3.3 Documentation: Your Future Self Will Thank You

3.3.1 What to Document

Design Intent: - Why this topology? - Why these component values? - Trade-offs considered - Rejected alternatives

Simulation Results: - All key plots (gain, PAE, ACLR vs power/frequency) - Parameter sweeps that guided choices - Sensitivity analysis

Test Data: - Raw data files (S2P, spectrum screenshots) - Test conditions (temperature, supply voltage) - Comparison to simulation (with explanation of differences)

Failures & Fixes: - What went wrong - How it was debugged - What was changed - Lessons learned

3.3.2 Tools & Templates

Lab Notebook (Physical or OneNote/Evernote) - Date-stamped entries - Sketch diagrams - Quick observations - “To investigate” list

Design Report (Word/Confluence) - Formal documentation - Architecture → Design → Test → Conclusion - 10-20 pages typical - Includes all figures and tables

Git Repository: - Schematic files (ADS, AWR) - Layout files (Altium, Cadence) - Scripts (Python, MATLAB) - Simulation data - Commit messages: Descriptive!

Example Commit:

"Modified output matching: Changed C5 from 4.7pF to 6.2pF to improve PAE at 3.8GHz band edge. PAE improved from 58% to 61% (+3%). ACLR still meets spec (-48 dBc). See Lab Notebook 2026-01-15 for details."

3.3.3 Documentation Time Investment

Guideline: 20% of project time on documentation - 12-week project → ~2.5 weeks documentation - Seems like a lot, but: - Future projects go faster (don’t repeat mistakes) - Handover is smooth - 6 months later, you’ll remember what you did

4 Interview Preparation: Technical Deep-Dive

4.1 Common PA Design Interview Questions

This section provides detailed answers to questions frequently asked in RF PA design interviews, drawn from actual interview experiences.

4.1.1 Question 1: Design a 40W PA for 3.5 GHz 5G Base Station

Full Answer:

Step 1: Requirements Clarification - Frequency: 3.4-3.8 GHz (n78 band) - Output power: 40W (46 dBm) - Efficiency target: >50% PAE at 6 dB backoff - Linearity: ACLR < -45 dBc (5G NR 100 MHz, 256-QAM)

Step 2: Technology & Topology Selection - Technology: GaN-on-SiC - Power density: 6-8 W/mm at 3.5 GHz - Required periphery: 40W / 6 W/mm = 6.7 mm - Select: 8 mm device (margin for process variation)

  • Topology: Doherty PA
    • Meets efficiency at backoff requirement
    • Proven technology for this application
    • Alternative (ET) too complex for first pass

Step 3: Device Selection - Qorvo QPD1013: 10 mm GaN device (popular choice) - Or Wolfspeed CGH40010F - Datasheet: 50W typical, 65% PAE - Load-pull data available

Step 4: Architecture - Asymmetric Doherty (2:1 ratio) - Main: 5 mm equivalent - Peak: 2.5 mm equivalent - Target 4.8 dB backoff efficiency peak

Step 5: Matching Network Design - Load-pull shows \(Z_{opt}\) = 8+j5 Ω @ 3.5 GHz - Output match: 8Ω → 50Ω (transformer ratio 6.25:1) - Three-section matching for BW (3.4-3.8 GHz) - Harmonic traps at 2f₀, 3f₀

Step 6: Doherty Network - Offset line (quarter-wave): \(Z_0 = \sqrt{8 \times 50} = 20Ω\) - Electrical length: 90° @ 3.5 GHz - Microstrip on RO4350B: ~12mm physical length

Step 7: Bias - Main: Class AB (\(V_{GS}\) = -2.5V, \(I_{DQ}\) = 100 mA) - Peak: Deep Class C (\(V_{GS}\) = -3.2V, \(I_{DQ}\) < 10 mA) - Supply: 28V

Step 8: Simulation - Harmonic balance in ADS - Load-pull sweep to optimize - Predict: 43 dBm, 60% PAE @ 6 dB backoff

Step 9: Layout & Fabrication - 2-layer PCB, RO4350B substrate - EM simulate output matching - Assemble, test, tune

Expected Performance: - P_out: 40-42W - PAE: 52-55% @ 6 dB backoff - ACLR: -40 dBc (without DPD), -50 dBc (with DPD)

Timeline: 10-12 weeks

4.1.2 Question 2: Your PA is Oscillating. How Do You Debug?

Systematic Approach:

Step 1: Characterize the Oscillation - Frequency: Spectrum analyzer - In-band → likely matching/stability issue - Out-of-band → bias network resonance - Multiple frequencies → parametric oscillation

  • Power Dependence: Does it occur at all power levels?
    • Only at high power → thermal or large-signal instability
    • Always → fundamental stability issue
  • Temperature Dependence: Heat/cool the PA
    • Temperature-dependent → thermal coupling or bias shift

Step 2: Isolate the Stage - If multi-stage PA, power down stages one at a time - Identify which stage is oscillating

Step 3: Check Obvious Issues - Power supply decoupling: Add more capacitors - Input termination: Is driver properly matched? - Bias stability: Check bias network impedance

Step 4: Stability Analysis - Measure S-parameters (small-signal) - Calculate K-factor vs frequency - If K < 1, identify the frequency range

Step 5: Quick Fixes to Try - Add series resistor (47-100Ω) on input - Shunt resistor (1kΩ) across bias inductor - Change bias inductance value (move self-resonance)

Step 6: Root Cause & Permanent Fix - Once stabilized, identify why it was unstable - Implement proper solution in design - Verify K > 1 from DC to 10×f₀

Story Time: In one project, PA oscillated only when someone walked past the lab bench (true story!). Root cause: Poor input match + ambient RF (WiFi) coupling into input. Fixed with 10dB attenuator at input (band-aid) and proper input matching in next revision.

4.1.3 Question 3: Explain Load-Pull and How You Use It

Complete Answer:

What is Load-Pull?

Load-pull is a measurement technique to characterize power amplifier performance as a function of load impedance.

Why Needed?

  • Device datasheet gives typical performance at 50Ω
  • But optimal impedance for max power is NOT 50Ω (typically 5-20Ω for RF power devices)
  • Load-pull sweeps load impedance to find \(Z_{opt}\)

Setup: - Source tuner: Presents controlled source impedance to device input - Load tuner: Presents controlled load impedance to device output - Measurement: Power meter, spectrum analyzer - Sweep: Move load impedance around Smith chart, measure performance

Measurements: 1. Max Power Contours: Find \(Z_{opt,power}\) 2. Max PAE Contours: Find \(Z_{opt,PAE}\) (often slightly different!) 3. ACLR Contours: Find impedance for best linearity

Interpretation:

Load-pull produces Smith chart with contour lines: - Each contour = constant performance (e.g., 40W, 41W, 42W) - Optimal impedance = center of innermost contour - Impedance sensitivity = how tightly spaced contours are - Tight → sensitive device, matching must be precise - Wide → tolerant device, easier to match

Design Use:

  1. From Load-Pull Data:
    • Extract \(Z_{opt}\) @ center frequency
    • Check \(Z_{opt}\) @ band edges (3.4 GHz, 3.8 GHz)
    • If different, need wideband matching
  2. Matching Network Design:
    • Transformer: \(Z_{opt}\) → 50Ω
    • Verify in simulation that device sees \(Z_{opt}\) ±10%
  3. Trade-off Analysis:
    • \(Z_{opt,power}\) vs \(Z_{opt,PAE}\) often differ by 10-20%
    • Choose based on priority (usually compromise between both)

Frequency Dependence:

\(Z_{opt}\) changes with frequency! For wideband PA: - Load-pull at 5-7 frequencies across band - Matching must present close to optimal across all

Harmonic Load-Pull:

Advanced technique: - Fix fundamental impedance, sweep 2f₀ impedance - Find harmonic termination for max PAE - Typically short circuit at harmonics is good starting point

4.1.4 Question 4: How Would You Improve Efficiency of an Existing PA?

Comprehensive Answer (in order of impact):

1. Re-characterize Optimal Load Impedance (High Impact) - Actual \(Z_{opt}\) may differ from initial design - Re-measure load-pull (if system available) - Adjust output matching for true optimal - Expected Gain: 3-8% PAE improvement

2. Bias Optimization (Medium-High Impact) - Sweep gate/base voltage, measure PAE - Often find better bias than default - Trade-off: May impact linearity - Expected Gain: 2-5% PAE improvement

3. Harmonic Termination (Medium Impact) - Measure/simulate impedance at 2f₀, 3f₀ - Add harmonic traps if needed - Short circuit at harmonics generally good for Class AB - Expected Gain: 1-3% PAE improvement

4. Drive Level Optimization (Medium Impact) - Adjust input power to optimal point - Too low: Device not compressed, poor PAE - Too high: Excessive IM distortion, possible damage - Expected Gain: 2-4% PAE improvement

5. Thermal Management (Medium Impact) - Reduce junction temperature - Lower temp → lower knee voltage, higher PAE - Better heatsink, improved airflow - Expected Gain: 1-3% PAE (if previously thermal-limited)

6. Doherty or ET Architecture (Very High Impact, but Re-Design) - If currently Class AB, convert to Doherty - Or implement envelope tracking - Requires significant design effort - Expected Gain: 15-25% PAE improvement at backoff

7. DPD Integration (Indirect) - DPD improves linearity, not efficiency directly - But: With better linearity, can operate at higher compression → higher PAE - Expected Gain: 3-7% PAE through operating point shift

Practical Example:

PAM_B tuning campaign improved PAE from 55% to 61%: - Re-matching (BOM variant J08): +3% PAE - Bias optimization: +2% PAE - Harmonic trap addition: +1% PAE

Total: +6% PAE through tuning (no re-design)

4.1.5 Question 5: Difference Between Class A, AB, B, C, E, F?

Complete Comparison:

Table 6.2: PA Class Comparison
Class Conduction Angle Max η Linearity Harmonics Typical Use Key Feature
A 360° 50% Excellent Low Low power, lab Always on, linear
AB 180-360° 50-78% Good Moderate Base station, handset Compromise
B 180° 78% Moderate High Not common (AB better) Half-wave current
C <180° 78-90% Poor Very High FM transmitter (constant env) Burst current, efficient
E ~50% 100% (theory) Poor Controlled Switched-mode PS Switch-mode, reactive load
F ~50% 88% Poor Controlled Research, specialized Harmonic tuning

Detailed Explanation:

Class A: - Device ON for full 360° of RF cycle - Current never goes to zero - Perfect linearity (current proportional to input) - Max efficiency: 50% (half power dissipated at peak) - Use: Low-power amplifiers, reference designs

Class AB: - Conduction angle: 180° < θ < 360° - Compromise between A (linear) and B (efficient) - Most common for RF power amps - Efficiency: 50-65% typical - With careful design (deep AB → near Class B): 65-75%

Class B: - Conduction angle: exactly 180° (half-wave) - Device OFF for half the cycle - Theoretical efficiency: 78.5% - Practical: 70-75% (due to knee voltage) - Rarely used alone (too distorted)

Class C: - Conduction angle: <180° (typically 120-150°) - High efficiency (80-85%) but very nonlinear - Generates strong harmonics (must filter) - Use: FM transmitters (constant envelope), frequency multipliers

Class E: - Switch-mode operation (ON/OFF, not linear) - Device acts as switch, not current source - Load network shapes voltage/current to minimize overlap - Theoretical efficiency: 100% (no V×I overlap) - Practical: 80-90% - Challenge: Switch frequency limited, harmonics

Class F: - Harmonic tuning to shape waveforms - Idea: Short at even harmonics, open at odd harmonics - Creates square wave voltage, half-sine current - Theoretical efficiency: 88% (Class F), 90% (Inverse F) - Practical: 75-85% - Challenge: Difficult to implement multi-harmonic tuning

Interview Follow-Up Questions:

  • Q: “Why is Class AB most common?”

  • A: Best compromise between efficiency and linearity. Can meet modern modulation requirements (with DPD) while achieving 60-70% PAE.

  • Q: “When would you use Class C?”

  • A: Only for constant-envelope signals (FM, FSK). Never for QAM or OFDM (too nonlinear, DPD can’t compensate).

  • Q: “Why not always use Class E for max efficiency?”

  • A: Switch-mode requires very fast transitions (high dV/dt, dI/dt). Difficult at GHz frequencies. Also very sensitive to load impedance variation. Good for low-freq (~MHz), challenging at microwave.

4.1.6 Question 6: Explain Doherty PA Operation

(See Chapter 5 Section 5.1.1 for full mathematical treatment. Interview version below)

Elevator Pitch (30 seconds):

Doherty is an efficiency enhancement technique using load modulation. Two amplifiers: main (always on) and peak (turns on at high power). At low power, main amp sees high impedance (2×Z_opt) and stays efficient. At high power, peak amp turns on, its current reduces main’s load impedance to Z_opt. Result: High efficiency maintained from 6 dB backoff to peak power.

Detailed Explanation (2-3 minutes):

Problem Doherty Solves: - Modern signals (5G) have high PAPR (9-12 dB) - Average power is 8-10 dB below peak - Class AB efficiency great at peak (65%), but terrible at backoff (30%) - Doherty maintains ~50% efficiency at backoff

How It Works:

Low Power (<6 dB backoff): - Main amplifier: Active, saturated - Peak amplifier: OFF (biased below threshold) - Load modulation network (quarter-wave line) presents high impedance to main - Main amp operates efficiently despite low output power

High Power (>6 dB backoff to peak): - Main amplifier: Saturated, constant output - Peak amplifier: Turns ON, begins contributing power - Peak amp current flows through combining network - Impedance transformation: Main amp’s load impedance decreases - At peak power: Both amps see Z_opt, both deliver rated power

Key Component: Impedance Inverter (quarter-wave transmission line) - Transforms impedances - Enables load modulation - Critical: Must be λ/4 at center frequency (limits bandwidth)

Design Parameters: - Main : Peak device ratio (1:1 symmetric, or 2:1 asymmetric) - Bias: Main = Class AB, Peak = Class C - Offset line impedance: \(Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_{opt} \times R_L}\)

Trade-offs: - ✅ Pros: 50% PAE at 6 dB backoff (vs 30% for Class AB), proven technology - ❌ Cons: Bandwidth limited (10-20%), complex impedance network, phase alignment critical

Interview Follow-Up:

  • Q: “Why quarter-wave line?”

  • A: Inverts impedance. High impedance at device becomes low at combining point, and vice versa. Allows main amp to see varying load impedance as peak turns on.

  • Q: “Why does it have limited bandwidth?”

  • A: Quarter-wave line is only λ/4 at one frequency. At other frequencies, electrical length changes, impedance transformation degrades, load modulation corrupted.

  • Q: “How to extend bandwidth?”

  • A: Modified offset line (non-uniform), dual-band design, or digital Doherty with tunable matching.

4.1.7 Question 7: How Do You Design Matching Networks?

Complete Design Process:

Step 1: Determine Target Impedances - Input: Usually 50Ω (system impedance) - Output (Device): From load-pull data - Typical: 5-20Ω for power devices - Example: \(Z_{opt}\) = 10+j5Ω - Output (System): 50Ω

Step 2: Calculate Transformation Ratio - Impedance transformation: \(r = \frac{R_L}{R_S}\) - Example: \(r = \frac{50}{10} = 5:1\)

Step 3: Choose Topology

For narrowband (<10% fractional BW):

  1. L-section:
    • Two components (L + C)
    • Simple, low loss
    • Q fixed by impedance ratio
    • No bandwidth control
  2. Pi or T-network:
    • Three components
    • Q controllable (adjust bandwidth)
    • More flexibility

For wideband (>10% fractional BW):

  1. Multi-section Matching:
    • Cascade of 2-4 L-sections
    • Each section transforms partial ratio
    • Example: 50Ω → 25Ω → 12Ω → 6Ω (for 50→6Ω total)
    • Wider bandwidth, more components
  2. Transmission Line Matching:
    • Quarter-wave transformers
    • \(Z_{TL} = \sqrt{Z_1 \times Z_2}\)
    • Example: 50Ω → 22.4Ω → 10Ω (using two sections)

Step 4: Design Calculations

Example: 10Ω → 50Ω using L-section at 3.5 GHz

Series-Parallel L-Match: \[Q = \sqrt{\frac{R_L}{R_S} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{50}{10} - 1} = 2.0\]

\[X_L = Q \times R_S = 2.0 \times 10 = 20Ω\] \[L = \frac{X_L}{2\pi f} = \frac{20}{2\pi \times 3.5 \times 10^9} = 0.91 \text{ nH}\]

\[X_C = \frac{R_L}{Q} = \frac{50}{2.0} = 25Ω\] \[C = \frac{1}{2\pi f X_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.5 \times 10^9 \times 25} = 1.82 \text{ pF}\]

Step 5: Simulation & Optimization - Build network in ADS/AWR Microwave Office - S-parameter simulation - Optimize for: - S11 < -15 dB (input match) - S21 gain (minimize loss) - Bandwidth (S11 < -10 dB across band)

Step 6: Consider Practical Factors

  • Component Availability: 1.82 pF → use 2.2 pF (closest standard value)
  • Component Q: Real inductors/capacitors have loss
    • Use high-Q RF components (Coilcraft, Murata)
    • Typical Q: 30-50 for inductors, 100-200 for capacitors
  • Parasitics: See earlier section on self-resonance
  • Layout: Microstrip line inductance, via inductance

Step 7: EM Simulation (for >2 GHz) - Model matching network in HFSS/Momentum - Include: PCB stack-up, vias, bondwires - Export S-parameters → import to circuit simulator

Step 8: Prototype & Tune - Build, measure S-parameters - Tune component values if needed - Typical adjustments: ±20% from initial design

Interview Follow-Up:

  • Q: “How do you match complex impedances (with imaginary part)?”

  • A: Absorb reactance into matching network. If \(Z_{opt} = 10+j5Ω\), first resonate out j5Ω with opposite reactance (-j5Ω), then match real 10Ω → 50Ω.

  • Q: “How to ensure wideband match?”

  • A: Use multi-section matching. Each section has lower Q (wider BW). Optimize section impedances for flat response across band.

4.2 Behavioral Interview Questions

4.2.1 “Tell me about a project failure and what you learned”

STAR Method (Situation, Task, Action, Result):

Situation: Designing a GaN PA for 5G small cell application. Tight schedule (8 weeks), aggressive specs (60% PAE at 6 dB backoff).

Task: My responsibility was matching network design and achieving efficiency target.

Action: - Designed Doherty PA based on simulation - Looked great in ADS: 62% PAE predicted - Skipped EM simulation (to save time) - Built prototype, measured only 52% PAE

Problem: - 10% efficiency gap unacceptable - Root cause analysis: Microstrip layout had significant parasitic inductance not modeled - Offset line electrical length was 95° instead of 90° → load modulation corrupted

Action Taken: - Re-ran full EM simulation (HFSS) - Found 0.8 nH via inductance at combining point - Adjusted offset line length to compensate - Fabricated Rev B

Result: - Rev B achieved 59% PAE (within spec) - Project delayed by 3 weeks - Cost: $8K in extra fabrication + lab time

Lesson Learned: > “Never skip EM simulation above 2 GHz. The time saved is illusory - you pay it back in debug and re-spins. Now I always do EM for critical sections, catches 90% of layout issues before fabrication.”

Additional Insight: This failure taught me the value of design validation steps. I now have a checklist: - ✅ EM simulation of passives - ✅ Thermal simulation - ✅ Stability analysis (DC to 10×f₀) - ✅ Design review with senior engineer

These checks add 1 week but prevent 3-week re-spins.

4.2.2 “Describe a time you had to make a difficult technical trade-off”

Situation: PAM_B project, late in tuning phase. Customer wanted both: 1. Linearity: ACLR < -48 dBc (tight!) 2. Efficiency: PAE > 58% at 6 dB backoff

Problem: Achieving both simultaneously very difficult.

Analysis: - Optimizing for linearity (bias towards Class A): PAE = 54%, ACLR = -52 dBc ✅ linearity, ❌ efficiency - Optimizing for efficiency (bias towards Class B): PAE = 61%, ACLR = -44 dBc ✅ efficiency, ❌ linearity - Compromise: PAE = 58%, ACLR = -47 dBc (barely met both)

Trade-Off Decision: - Evaluated system-level impact: - Linearity: Hard requirement (regulatory, can’t relax) - Efficiency: Nice-to-have (affects thermal design) - Discussed with system engineer and customer - Decision: Prioritize linearity, accept 57% PAE (1% below target)

Mitigation: - Implemented DPD to improve linearity → relaxed PA linearity requirement - With DPD: Could operate PA at higher compression - Final result: PAE = 59% (met!), ACLR = -52 dBc (exceeded!)

Lesson Learned: > “Understand system-level priorities. Component specs are negotiable if system performance is met. Also, DPD is a powerful tool - factor it into design from the start.”

5 Career Development in PA Design

5.1 From Junior to Senior Engineer

5.1.1 Junior Engineer (0-3 years)

Focus: Learn fundamentals, execute tasks

Typical Responsibilities: - Perform simulations under guidance - Build prototypes, conduct tests - Document results - Support senior engineers

Skills to Develop: - Tools: ADS, Microwave Office, HFSS - Measurements: VNA, spectrum analyzer, power meter - Theory: Smith chart, matching networks, stability

How to Excel: - Ask “why” (understand rationale, not just execute) - Document thoroughly (builds your reference library) - Read application notes (Qorvo, Wolfspeed have excellent GaN AN) - Join IEEE, attend conferences (IMS, EuMW)

Timeline: 2-3 years to reach mid-level

5.1.2 Mid-Level Engineer (3-7 years)

Focus: Design ownership, solve problems independently

Typical Responsibilities: - Own PA module design end-to-end - Debug complex issues - Trade-off analysis and decision-making - Mentor junior engineers

Skills to Develop: - Architecture: Choose topology, device, approach - Debugging: Systematic problem-solving - Communication: Present at design reviews - Project Management: Timelines, risk mitigation

How to Excel: - Seek challenging projects (learn most from difficult designs) - Lead design reviews (forces you to think deeply) - Publish (IEEE papers, internal whitepapers) - Build cross-functional relationships (system, mechanical, software)

Timeline: 4-5 years to reach senior level

5.1.3 Senior Engineer (7-12 years)

Focus: Innovation, mentorship, architecture

Typical Responsibilities: - Define PA architecture for new products - Advanced techniques (Doherty, ET, DPD) - Mentor team, train engineers - Technical leadership across projects - Customer/executive presentations

Skills to Develop: - Innovation: Novel solutions, patents - System Thinking: How PA fits in transceiver, base station - Leadership: Influence without authority - Strategy: Technology roadmap, make vs buy

How to Excel: - Publish externally (IEEE Trans, conferences) - File patents (demonstrate innovation) - Teach (internal courses, university collaboration) - Stay current (follow research, GaN roadmap)

Beyond Senior: Principal Engineer, Fellow (technical track), or Engineering Manager (leadership track)

5.2 Skills Beyond RF Design

Successful PA Engineers Need:

  1. Electromagnetic (EM) Fundamentals
    • Maxwell’s equations (conceptual understanding)
    • Transmission line theory
    • Waveguide modes, substrate modes
  2. Thermal Engineering
    • Heat transfer (conduction, convection, radiation)
    • Thermal simulation (Icepak, Flotherm)
    • Heatsink design
  3. Digital Signal Processing
    • For DPD implementation
    • I/Q modulation, upconversion/downconversion
    • Filter design
  4. Mechanical Integration
    • Tolerancing, manufacturing processes
    • Collaborate with mechanical team
    • Understand assembly constraints
  5. Software/Scripting
    • Python, MATLAB for automation
    • Data analysis, plotting
    • Design optimization scripts
  6. Communication
    • Write clear design reports
    • Present to executives (non-technical audience)
    • Cross-functional collaboration

5.3 Continuous Learning

Stay Current:

  • Conferences: IEEE IMS (June), EuMW (Sept), APMC (Dec)
  • Journals: IEEE Trans. Microwave Theory & Tech, IEEE Microwave Magazine
  • Courses: IEEE, Besser Associates workshops
  • Vendor Training: Qorvo, Wolfspeed, Keysight offer free webinars

Recommended Books:

  1. Steve Cripps, “RF Power Amplifiers for Wireless Communications” (2nd Ed.)
  2. Peter Vizmuller, “RF Design Guide: Systems, Circuits, and Equations”
  3. Guillermo Gonzalez, “Microwave Transistor Amplifiers” (analysis and design)
  4. F.H. Raab et al., “Power Amplifiers and Transmitters for RF and Microwave” (IEEE survey paper)

Certifications: Not critical, but consider: - IEEE Senior Member (after 10 years) - PE license (if working on military/aerospace)

6 Summary & Final Wisdom

6.1 Key Principles

Design Philosophy:

  1. Simulation guides, measurement validates - Always prototype
  2. Design for margin - Murphy’s law applies to PA design
  3. Thermal design is not optional - Cold PAs work, hot PAs fail
  4. Document everything - Your future self will thank you
  5. Understand the “why” - Don’t blindly follow cookbook

Debugging Mindset:

  1. Systematic approach - Don’t randomly change things
  2. One variable at a time - Isolate root cause
  3. Measure, don’t guess - Use instruments, trust data
  4. Check the obvious first - Bias voltages, connections, power supply
  5. Learn from failures - Best lessons come from broken PAs

Career Success:

  1. Continuous learning - Technology evolves rapidly
  2. Build relationships - Collaborate across disciplines
  3. Teach others - Best way to solidify your knowledge
  4. Stay curious - Always ask “why” and “what if”
  5. Balance breadth and depth - Expert in PA, competent in system

6.2 Final Thoughts

Power amplifier design is a beautiful intersection of theory, intuition, and craftsmanship. It’s challenging because it demands:

  • Deep theory: Maxwell’s equations to system architecture
  • Practical skills: Soldering, lab instruments, debugging
  • Cross-disciplinary knowledge: RF, thermal, mechanical, digital
  • Art and science: Optimization is often non-intuitive

But it’s also incredibly rewarding when you: - First see your PA hit target power and efficiency - Ship a design that powers millions of 5G connections - Mentor a junior engineer who becomes expert - Innovate a technique that advances the field

“In theory, there is no difference between theory and practice. In practice, there is.”
— Yogi Berra (applies perfectly to PA design!)

Good luck in your PA design journey! May your PAs be stable, efficient, and linear. 🚀

Chapter 6 Complete
End of Manual - Thank you for reading!

6.3 About the Author

This manual distills 12 years of RF power amplifier design experience, including:

  • 5G base station PAs (macro and small cell)
  • GaN technology development and characterization
  • Doherty PA architectures for massive MIMO
  • Envelope tracking systems
  • Digital pre-distortion algorithm development

Projects spanned frequencies from 700 MHz to 40 GHz, power levels from 1W to 200W, covering technologies from GaAs to GaN-on-SiC.

Contact: For questions or feedback on this manual, please reach out via GitHub repository issues.

Contributing: If you have lessons learned or case studies to add, contributions are welcome!
